philosophy: Izzet Spells is a high-tempo, spell-slinging archetype that wins by casting a high volume of instant and sorcery spells in a single turn to either deal direct damage via Stormchaser's Talent or power up threats like Slickshot Show-Off, often leveraging Flow State to dig for consistency and chain spells together. It excels against slower, resource-heavy decks that can't answer its burst damage or disrupt its spell density.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands and at least one spell-casting engine (Flow State, Slickshot Show-Off, or a cheap cantrip). Keep hands with multiple cheap instants/sorceries (1-2 mana) that enable Stormchaser's Talent or Show-Off. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, three+ lands with no early spells, or hands lacking any way to cast multiple spells in one turn.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap spell to set up the turn. If you have Flow State, play it on turn 1 or 2 if you have mana to cast a spell from your library. Turn 2: Cast a second spell, ideally a cantrip or removal, to build spell count. If you have Slickshot Show-Off, cast it on turn 2 or 3 after casting 1-2 spells to make it a meaningful threat. Always prioritize casting spells over holding up mana for counterspells unless facing a known control deck.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in countermagic, spot removal, and life gain. Against control/midrange: Bring in more cantrips, card advantage, and direct damage. Against mirror: Bring in additional counterspells, discard, and hate cards against Flow State. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't dilute your core game plan.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your library as a secondary hand when Flow State is active. Prioritize casting instants/sorceries over creatures to maximize spell count for Stormchaser's Talent or Show-Off. Don't overextend creatures if you can win by casting spells. Manage your mana to ensure you can cast your win condition in a single turn; hold priority to chain spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit creatures like Slickshot Show-Off only when you can protect them or when you're ready to swing for lethal. Use removal sparingly; prefer to out-value opponents by casting more spells. If the board is clear, focus on digging for Flow State or Stormchaser's Talent. If the opponent has a board, use spells to clear it while building your own spell count.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up mana or cast in response to their spells), sweepers (have a way to protect your win condition or win before it resolves), and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for big spells; that's your window to cast multiple spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Stormchaser's Talent is the primary finisher; cast it after 3+ spells in a turn for lethal. Slickshot Show-Off can close the game if it's large enough and unblocked. Flow State enables infinite or semi-infinite spell chains if paired with draw engines or cheap cantrips.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Flow State on the battlefield, enough mana to cast 4+ spells, and a clear path to lethal. Also commit when the opponent has no interaction left or is at low life.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock with no way to break it, or if you're down to your last card and the opponent has a board wipe ready. Also concede if you're facing a combo deck that will win before you can cast your spells, and you have no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells to trade with counters. Cast your win condition in response to their spells, or use multiple spells to force multiple counters. Bring in more cantrips or discard to remove their counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Slickshot Show-Off and cantrips. Don't let them get to their big mana; use spot removal and discard to disrupt their land drops. If they ramp, win quickly with Stormchaser's Talent before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play defensively early with cantrips and spot removal. Don't overcommit to spell chains if you'll die to combat. Use Flow State to dig for answers or a quick win. Prioritize survival over combo.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind them with card advantage and spell density. Use discard to remove their key answers. Chain spells to pressure their life total while they try to find removal. If they have sweepers, wait for them to use it, then cast your win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the spell war by having better sequencing and more interaction. Use discard to remove their Flow State or Stormchaser's Talent. Hold up counterspells for their win conditions. Prioritize casting spells in response to their spells to maximize your own spell count.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your spell density based on the metagame. Against control-heavy fields, add more card draw and discard to dig through your deck. Against aggro, reduce spell density slightly in favor of interaction and life gain. In mirrors, prioritize hate cards against Flow State and additional counterspells. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't dilute your core game plan; keep at least 2 copies of your primary win conditions.
notes: Flow State is your engine; never cast it unless you can immediately cast an instant or sorcery from your library. Stormchaser's Talent scales with the number of spells cast this turn, so sequence your spells carefully (e.g., cantrip -> removal -> Stormchaser's Talent). Slickshot Show-Off is vulnerable to sweepers; don't rely on it as your only win condition. Watch out for spell count math; each instant/sorcery you cast increases the damage or power/toughness, so plan your turns accordingly. Avoid overcommitting to a single turn if you have no way to protect your win condition.

philosophy: Selesnya Landfall is an aggressive, resource-advantage deck that wins by rapidly deploying lands to trigger massive creature pumps and overwhelming the board with giant 1/1s. It excels in fast, interactive metagames where it can race spell-slingers and out-value control with relentless land drops and efficient card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for mana fixing and Erode targets), plus at least one of Badgermole Cub or Mightform Harmonizer. Keep hands with Erode if paired with a land or creature. Mulligan to 4+ lands, or hands with multiple spells and no lands. Prioritize hands that can play a land on turn 1 and a creature/land on turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land. Turn 2: Play a second land, then cast Badgermole Cub or Mightform Harmonizer. If you have Erode, use it to fetch a basic land to trigger landfall and immediately pump your creature. Sequence lands first to maximize trigger counts, then cast creatures to capitalize on immediate landfall triggers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in targeted removal, bounce effects, and additional Erode to disrupt spell-heavy strategies. vs Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in efficient removal, card advantage engines, and hate cards against opposing landfall synergies. vs Control/Combo: Bring in more threats, protection spells, and graveyard hate if applicable. General: 2-4 removal, 2-4 land acceleration/fetches, 2-3 hate cards depending on meta.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize land drops above all else; each land is a trigger and a resource. Use Erode strategically to fetch basics for mana fixing or to trigger landfall at optimal times. Don't overcommit to hand if you can stabilize with landfall triggers. Manage your graveyard carefully if the format has graveyard hate, but generally, landfall doesn't rely heavily on it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit creatures once you have 2+ lands on the battlefield to ensure immediate pump triggers. Hold back if the opponent has hard removal or sweepers; use Erode to bounce threats or fetch lands to stabilize. Don't overextend into potential board wipes unless you have a clear win condition or can follow up with more landfall triggers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers (like Wrath of God), and targeted removal. Note when opponents tap out for spells vs. holding mana for interaction. Track their land count to predict if they can cast their win conditions. Pay attention to when they use removal on your pump creatures vs. your lands.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Mightform Harmonizer with multiple landfall triggers, or a fully pumped Badgermole Cub. Win by attacking with giant creatures or using Erode to fetch lands that trigger additional pumps. If behind, use land drops to trigger enough landfall to stabilize and counter-attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ lands on the battlefield, your creatures are pumped, and the opponent has no sweepers or hard removal in hand. Also commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns of combat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no way to break through, if you're down to 1-2 lands with no creatures and no draw spells, or if the opponent has established a board wipe + finisher combo that you can't answer. Also concede if you're consistently drawing lands with no creatures or vice versa.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Erode to fetch lands and trigger landfall before casting threats, or cast creatures in response to their spells. Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter. If possible, use bounce or protection to protect key creatures from targeted counters or removal.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Outpace them with aggressive land drops and early threats. Use Erode to disrupt their mana base or fetch lands to stabilize. Don't let them get too far ahead; pressure early and often to force mistakes or win before their late-game threats resolve.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early land drops and pump creatures to trade efficiently. Use Erode to bounce key threats or fetch lands to trigger landfall and stabilize the board. Don't overcommit to a wide board if they have sweepers; focus on trading 1-for-1 efficiently.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with landfall triggers. Use Erode to disrupt their mana or fetch lands to keep triggering. Don't let them stabilize completely; keep attacking and threatening. If they have sweepers, wait for the right moment to commit or use bounce to protect key creatures.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource race. Prioritize card advantage and efficient removal. Use Erode to disrupt their land drops or fetch lands to trigger landfall at critical moments. Track their hand to know when they have removal. Win by out-resourcing them with better sequencing and sideboard choices.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your threat/reduction ratio based on the metagame. Against spell-heavy decks, bring in more interaction and protection. Against mirrors, prioritize card advantage and targeted removal. Always ensure you have enough basic lands in your main deck or sideboard to support Erode and mana fixing. Adjust your land count if you're consistently flooding or screwing.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to a wide board without accounting for sweepers. Always count the opponent's potential removal. Another mistake: Not using Erode optimally for landfall triggers. Remember that fetching a land triggers landfall, so you can pump creatures before they even enter the battlefield. Also, be mindful of format-specific hate cards if applicable, though Landfall is less graveyard-dependent. Practice sequencing land drops to maximize trigger counts.

philosophy: Izzet Spellementals is a midrange-control hybrid that wins by leveraging instant and sorcery synergy to incrementally grow threats, control the board with cheap interaction, and out-value opponents through card advantage and spell-driven triggers. It excels against control and midrange decks that overcommit to static boards, but requires careful tempo management to survive early aggression.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (ensuring at least one U and one R source), 3-4 spells (instants/sorceries), and at least one 2-drop spellmentals creature. Prioritize hands with 1-mana cantrips or cheap removal to stabilize. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no early spells, or only high-cost threats without fuel.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a 1-mana cantrip or cheap spell if mana allows. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a 1-2 mana spellmentals creature (e.g., Eddymurk Crab), and immediately follow up with an instant/sorcery to trigger its growth ability. Sequence lands to guarantee U/R availability; if opening with a single color source, consider mulliganing unless you have a cantrip to fix mana.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in cheap removal, counterspells, and early board wipes to stabilize. vs Control: Bring in additional threats, card draw, and uncounterable spells to out-grind. vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, counterspells, and fast finishers. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells, and card advantage to win the spell war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting spells for triggers and developing threats. Avoid emptying your hand unless you have a clear path to victory. Use the graveyard as a resource for recursion or alternative wins, but prioritize keeping key interaction in hand. Track your spell count to maximize spellmentals growth.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or counterspells to protect them from sweepers or removal. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered interaction or is building a wide board. Use instants/sorceries to clear blockers or answer threats before committing to combat.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., board wipes), counterspells, and fast aggro threats. Note when the opponent saves removal vs. when they commit to a sweeper. Track their graveyard if recursion is a threat, and monitor their mana for key combo pieces or finishers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large spellmentals with trample or flying, fueled by a full graveyard and hand of cheap spells. Use cheap instants/sorceries to close out games, either through combat or direct damage. If the opponent is low on interaction, go wide with pumped threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, protection (counters/removal), and the opponent is low on interaction. Commit when you can trade efficiently or when the opponent is forced to use their last interaction on a non-critical threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has a hard lock (e.g., stax, graveyard recursion lock), you're out of resources, or the matchup is mathematically lost (e.g., vs. fast combo with no interaction, or vs. control with a guaranteed win condition). Don't overextend into a sweeper with no follow-up.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells to out-value counters, split your threats across multiple turns, and cast multiple spells in response to force counter trade-offs. If available, use uncounterable effects or flashback/graveyard recursion to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to punish their slow start. Use targeted removal on key ramp pieces (e.g., mana dorks, landfall enablers) and avoid overcommitting to a race. Stabilize with removal/counters, then transition to midrange grind.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal, counterspells, and early board wipes. Don't overextend into combat; use instants to clear blockers or answer threats. Transition to midrange once stabilized, using spellmentals to out-value their creatures.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and incremental pressure. Use spells to bypass their interaction, and don't let them stabilize with sweepers. Apply consistent pressure while holding up interaction for their key threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the spell war by tracking counterspells and prioritizing card advantage. Use targeted removal efficiently, and don't overcommit to the board. Force the opponent to use interaction on non-critical threats, then close out with a pumped spellmentals.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your game plan to the specific matchup. Against control/combo, increase interaction and card advantage while reducing low-impact threats. Against aggro/midrange, add more threats and early pressure. Generally, bring in matchup-specific hate, adjust land counts if needed, and ensure you have answers for the most common threats in the format.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting without protection, mismanaging mana for spells vs. creatures, and ignoring graveyard synergy. Unique interactions include stack manipulation to trigger multiple spellmentals, and using cheap spells to out-value counterspells. Always track the opponent's interaction and graveyard to maximize your spell-driven triggers.

philosophy: Izzet Prowess wins by casting multiple nonland spells per turn to trigger the Prowess ability, generating card advantage and direct damage while out-resourcing opponents through tempo and burn. It excels against slower, resource-heavy decks that struggle to answer a dense stream of cheap spells and direct damage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or mana rocks), at least one cheap cantrip or mana spell (e.g., Arcum's Astrolabe, Manamorphose), and a Prowess enabler or early damage spell. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no spells, or 0-1 lands with no cantrips/rituals to fix mana. Keep any hand with a turn-1 playable spell or mana accelerator.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or mana spell to trigger Prowess and dig. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a second spell to trigger Prowess again, aiming to draw into threats or burn. Prioritize playing lands that enter untapped or can produce multiple colors, and sequence spells to maximize Prowess triggers before committing to combat.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in direct damage (Lightning Bolt/Strike), artifact hate (Red Elemental Blast), and discard. vs Izzet Spells/Spellementals: Bring in counterspell hate (Red Elemental Blast, Abrade), more burn, and card advantage. vs Mirror: Bring in additional Prowess enablers, counterspell hate, and sideboard cards that disrupt spell chains (e.g., Red Elemental Blast, Flusterstorm if available).

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat hand as a resource; prioritize casting multiple spells per turn to trigger Prowess and dig deeper. Manage mana carefully to ensure you can cast 2+ spells on key turns. Use graveyard strategically if the deck utilizes it, but avoid overcommitting to graveyard synergies unless necessary.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing all threats if the opponent has removal or counterspells. Use Prowess triggers to scout and stabilize before going wide. If you have a clear advantage, commit burn and threats to close the game.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up mana for Prowess triggers or have backup plans), sweepers (prepare with artifact destruction or burn), and ramp threats (track mana development and prepare early pressure). Key tell: Opponent holding 2+ mana on their turn often signals a counter or removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Direct damage spells (Lightning Strike, Grapeshot, Aetherflux Reservoir) or combat damage from Prowess-enriched threats. Win by reducing opponent to 0 life through spell chains or combat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough Prowess triggers to draw into your finisher, or when the opponent is at lethal range (10 or less life). Commit when you can safely cast 3+ spells in a turn to ensure you draw through your deck.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no interaction, if your hand is dead against a specific hate card, or if you're down 3+ life with no Prowess enablers or burn in hand. Also concede if the opponent has established an unbreakable board and you lack removal.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Prowess to dig through counterspells, cast spells in response to triggers, or use cheap cantrips to find answers. Hold up mana for Prowess triggers to maintain tempo. Consider sideboarded artifact hate or counterspell disruption.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to punish their mana development. Use direct damage to remove their ramp sources or finish them before they stabilize. Track their land drops and prepare burn.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early Prowess triggers to draw into removal or burn. Use counterspells or discard to disrupt their early game. Prioritize survival over tempo in the first two turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind them with Prowess card advantage. Use cheap spells to trigger Prowess and dig past their answers. Apply consistent pressure to force them to use resources on interaction rather than their win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better spell sequencing, more efficient mana, and superior sideboard choices. Prioritize disrupting their spell chains with counterspell hate and direct damage. Track their hand size and Prowess triggers to out-draw them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your spell-to-land ratio based on matchup speed. Against slower decks, add more burn and card advantage; against faster decks, add early interaction and discard. Always prioritize keeping your Prowess engine intact while adding matchup-specific hate.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to combat without triggering Prowess first. Always cast spells before attacking to maximize Prowess triggers. Another: Ignoring mana screw; ensure your deck has enough mana acceleration and dual lands. Unique interaction: Prowess triggers on the stack, so you can cast multiple spells in response to each other to trigger Prowess repeatedly. Signature cards like Stormchaser's Talent and Flow State are turn-2/3 finishers that also trigger Prowess, so sequence them after cheaper enablers to maximize card draw and mana generation.

philosophy: Mono-Green Landfall aims to accelerate into a dense land base while deploying cheap, synergistic creatures that trigger repeatedly on land drops. It wins by out-valuing opponents through card advantage, board presence, and cumulative triggers, particularly excelling against slower control decks and resource wars like mirrors.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/accelerants), at least one landfall creature (Badgermole Cub, Icetill Explorer, or Mightform Harmonizer), and a land accelerator. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no creatures, or hands with only creatures and no land/acceleration. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a creature on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. If you have a cheap landfall creature and a land accelerator, consider playing the creature on turn 1 if you can drop a land on turn 2. Turn 2: Play a second land, then drop a landfall creature. Sequence lands to maximize triggers—play accelerants before creatures to trigger landfall immediately. If holding multiple lands, consider playing them all at once to trigger multiple landfall abilities.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in direct damage, counterspell hate, or early interaction. Against Selesnya Landfall: Bring in targeted removal, card draw, or land destruction. Against Control: Bring in more threats, card advantage, and ways to protect your board. Against Aggro/Combo: Bring in early interaction, board wipes, or clock enhancers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize land drops over hoarding cards. Use landfall triggers to filter your deck and find answers. Don't overcommit mana to non-essential spells; every land drop is a trigger. Manage your graveyard carefully if the format has graveyard hate, but generally, landfall doesn't rely heavily on it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough lands to trigger landfall repeatedly and can afford to trade. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal, and use landfall triggers to draw into answers. Protect your key enablers (like Mightform Harmonizer) with counters or removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (like Wrath of God variants), targeted removal (like Lightning Strike), and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for interaction. Track their land count to predict if they can answer your threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Mightform Harmonizer (or similar high-impact landfall creatures) combined with a full board of triggered creatures. Win by overwhelming the board with +1/+1 counters, card advantage, or direct damage from landfall triggers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 4+ lands in play, multiple landfall creatures on the board, and the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal in hand. Also commit when you can close the game before their control deck stabilizes.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard control deck with no way to break through, if you're down to one land and no creatures, or if the opponent has a guaranteed win condition (like a combo or infinite damage) that you can't disrupt.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter (e.g., 1-drops). Sequence land drops to trigger landfall before casting expensive spells. Use uncounterable effects or split your spells across turns.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-ramp them with more efficient land acceleration. Use targeted removal on their key ramp creatures. Don't get bogged down in a mana race; focus on board presence and triggers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and board wipes. Use landfall triggers to draw into answers quickly. Don't overextend into combat; trade efficiently and stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply early pressure to force them to use resources. Use landfall card draw to out-value their control elements. Protect your threats with counters or removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by drawing more cards and playing more lands. Prioritize card advantage and efficient removal. Protect your key landfall enablers and don't overcommit to the board unless you have a clear advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, add more late-game threats and card draw. Against aggro, add early interaction and clock enhancers. In mirrors, prioritize card advantage and targeted removal. Always keep your land count consistent; sideboarding shouldn't disrupt your landfall triggers.
notes: Landfall is highly sensitive to land drops—never skip a land play unless you're casting a game-winning spell. Practice sequencing land drops to maximize triggers. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection, as sweepers are common. Remember that Mightform Harmonizer and other key creatures scale with triggers, so prioritize casting them when you have multiple lands ready to drop.

philosophy: Izzet Lessons is a spell-slinger tempo-control hybrid that wins by leveraging cumulative spell-casting effects, card advantage, and resilient late-game threats. It aims to out-value opponents through spell triggers, copying, and incremental damage, beating down control and combo with relentless pressure while stabilizing against aggro with interaction and Monument to Endurance.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or shock-equivalents for fixing), 3+ spells, and at least one early interaction or mana accelerator. Prioritize hands containing Artist's Talent or Gran-Gran if mana supports them. Mulligan pure land hands, triple-colored mana without spells, or hands lacking early plays in faster formats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap cantrip or spell trigger to establish board presence and activate 'lesson' effects. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a mid-cost spell or threat, leveraging spell triggers for card advantage. Sequence lands to ensure color fixing without stalling; prioritize dual/shock lands if the format allows.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Landfall (Selesnya/Mono-Green): Bring in direct damage, spot removal, and early blockers. vs Control/Combo (Izzet Spells/Prowess): Bring in counterspells, card advantage engines, and hate bears. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counters, and late-game finishers to out-value.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction during opponent's turns. Use cantrips to maintain hand size and dig for key pieces. Don't overcommit to the board if the opponent has sweepers or heavy interaction. Track spell count carefully, as it fuels your primary win conditions.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or counterspells to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has potential sweepers or holds up mana for interaction. Use Monument to Endurance to stabilize against aggressive boards and buy time for your spell engine.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and combo pieces. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for interaction. Track spell count to anticipate trigger value and predict when you can safely commit to the board.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Gran-Gran as a late-game threat that scales with total spells cast, or copying effects via Artist's Talent to swing the board. Monument to Endurance provides the survivability needed to close out games against aggressive or midrange decks.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for key threats, and the opponent is low on interaction. Commit when spell triggers guarantee value on the stack and you can protect your board state.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold against locked-out control with no draw engines, or against aggro with no stabilization tools. Concede if you're dead to the board and have no way to interact or win, especially if your spell engine is disrupted.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap spells to force counter trade-offs. Employ protection, hexproof, or uncounterable effects. Cast spells in response to known counterspells to maximize value and pressure.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt land drops. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Stabilize with Monument to Endurance and clear the board when possible to slow their acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and spot removal. Use cantrips to dig for answers and stabilize with Monument to Endurance. Transition to value spells once the board is clear.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with card draw and spell triggers. Pressure the board to force mistakes and resource depletion. Use Artist's Talent to copy key spells or threats, overwhelming their interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through better resource management and sideboard choices. Prioritize targeted removal and counterspells. Out-value in the late game with Gran-Gran and spell triggers, leveraging hand size and board presence.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against slower decks, bring in more late-game finishers and card advantage. Against faster decks, prioritize early interaction and stabilization. Always keep your core spell-slinger engine intact, but tailor your interaction suite to the metagame.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without protection. Unique interaction: Gran-Gran's triggers scale with total spells cast, so don't forget to count your own cantrips and opponent's spells if they trigger. Artist's Talent can copy instant-speed spells for additional value. Monument to Endurance is your primary win condition enabler against aggressive decks.

philosophy: Dimir Excruciator is a control-tempo archetype that wins by out-valuing opponents through card advantage, precise interaction, and late-game finishers. It aims to neutralize fast spell and landfall strategies, stabilize the board, and close out games efficiently once resources are exhausted.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fixing), 1-2 early interaction spells, and 1-2 draw engines. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ lands and no spells, or only removal with no early play/draw. Keep any hand with a turn-1 land and a cantrip or cheap interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction if available. Turn 2: Develop board with a 2-drop threat or removal, ensuring mana base is online. Prioritize fixing over raw speed to reliably cast U/B spells on curve. Use early spells to probe for interaction and establish tempo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Landfall/Prowess): +2 spot removal/anti-aggro counters, +1 graveyard hate, -2 card draw. vs Control/Spells: +1 additional counterspells, +1 late-game value, -2 early removal. vs Mirror: +1 targeted removal, +1 hand disruption, -2 generic draw. Always track opponent sideboard trends and adjust accordingly.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until threats are committed. Use card draw to maintain hand size, but avoid overcommitting to one board state. Manage graveyard by sideboarding in hate if needed, but don't over-index on it early. Prioritize keeping interaction for the opponent's turn.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or counterspells in hand to protect them. Hold back on casting expensive finishers if the opponent has unanswered interaction. Trade efficiently: use removal on key threats, save counterspells for game-ending spells or combo pieces.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for landfall triggers, spell stacks, and graveyard recursion. Key tells: opponent holding up mana on their turn end suggests a counter or instant-speed interaction. Track how many lands they've played to gauge ramp potential and adjust your pressure accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact threats or combo pieces that can close the game in 1-2 turns. Use card advantage to ensure you have the final answer or the final blow. Signature cards like Stock Up and Superior Spider-Man should be deployed to generate advantage or stabilize, not as early-game threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, board control, and the opponent has no clear path to stabilize. Commit finishers when you can protect them or when the opponent is at lethal range. Only commit if you have a plan to handle their response.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single land, have no interaction, and the opponent has multiple threats or a proven win condition. Concede if your deck's primary win condition is completely locked out by the sideboard or if you're facing a mathematically unwinnable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig through counters, cast spells in response to their counters, or use uncounterable threats. Bait counters with cheap spells before committing your finisher. Prioritize stack control and force unfavorable trades.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt land drops, use spot removal on key ramp creatures, and sideboard in additional removal or counterspells. Don't let them stabilize into their late-game. Force them to interact with your threats instead of developing their board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap interaction and board wipes. Trade efficiently, don't overdraw, and stabilize before going for the win. Sideboard in more removal and less card draw. Focus on surviving the first 3 turns and out-value them in the midgame.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage, apply consistent pressure, and be ready to answer their sweepers. Don't overcommit to the board; maintain hand resources to respond to their control elements. Force them to spend resources on your threats rather than their game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, efficient removal, and hand disruption. Control the stack, force unfavorable trades, and sideboard in targeted hate for their key pieces. The player who manages their hand and board most efficiently will win.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and resource balance based on the metagame. Against faster decks, reduce card draw and increase interaction. Against control, add more value engines and late-game threats. Always track opponent sideboard trends and adjust your main deck strategy accordingly. Prioritize flexibility over raw power in sideboard slots.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without hand resources. Always keep interaction for the opponent's turn. Be mindful of mana screw in U/B; prioritize fixing lands. Signature cards should be used to generate advantage or stabilize, not as early-game threats. Practice stack sequencing and learn to read opponent mana states to maximize interaction efficiency.

philosophy: Stabilize the early game with efficient interaction and card filtering, then out-value opponents through superior card advantage and resilient threats. Excels against fast, combo, and ramp strategies by surviving their initial burst and grinding them down in the late game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 4-5 lands, at least one turn-1 or turn-2 interaction (Inevitable Defeat or cheap removal), and a draw spell (Stock Up or Jeskai Revelation) if you have 3+ lands. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early interaction, or 2 lands with no spells. Prioritize keeping hands that can play a land and cast a cantrip or answer on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, hold priority for interaction or cast a cheap cantrip if you have 2+ lands. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast your first interaction or draw spell. Sequence lands to ensure WU/B/R access by turn 3. Use Stock Up early to filter into answers or threats, but avoid overcommitting mana if the board is unstable.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Landfall/Prowess): +2 Inevitable Defeat, +1 board wipe, -1 Jeskai Revelation. Against Control/Spells: +2 Jeskai Revelation, +1 additional draw spell, -1 removal. Against Combo/Ramp: +2 graveyard hate, +1 hard removal, -1 card draw. Against Mirror: +1 Inevitable Defeat, +1 Jeskai Revelation, -1 generic interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoarding interaction is key; only spend removal on threats that can swing the game or enable opponent's win condition. Use Stock Up to cycle dead cards into answers or threats. Actively monitor and empty the opponent's graveyard with Jeskai Revelation to prevent recursion or combo enablers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have answered their primary interaction or when you can safely trade removal for their key piece. Hold back a removal spell for turn-3-4 finishers. If the opponent is going wide, prioritize board wipes or Inevitable Defeat on your own creatures to stabilize and push damage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard recursion (Selesnya Landfall), stack interaction (Izzet Spells/Prowess), and early damage sources. Key tells: Opponent holding up mana on turn 2-3 usually indicates a counter, removal, or finisher. Track how many removal spells they've seen to gauge their graveyard safety and board control capacity.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Resilient creatures and Soldier tokens generated by Inevitable Defeat, combined with Jeskai Revelation for massive card advantage and graveyard exile. Incremental pressure from tokens and efficient removal wins the race once the board is stabilized.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established board control, opponent has no sweepers or counters left, and you can safely cast Jeskai Revelation or a finisher without fear of being countered or removed. Commit to trading removal efficiently to clear the path for your threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 1-2 cards in hand, opponent has a guaranteed win condition (e.g., Landfall combo, Prowess finisher), and you have no removal, graveyard hate, or way to disrupt their graveyard. Also concede if you are completely out of resources and opponent has established an unbreakable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap spells and finishers to force counterspells, then capitalize on Jeskai Revelation to exile countered spells from the graveyard. Bypass counters with discard, uncounterable threats, or by casting spells in response to their counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with interaction to stop mana dorks and land drops. Use Jeskai Revelation to exile ramp spells from the graveyard if they recur. Prioritize board wipes and hard removal to stop their big threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Flood the board with cheap removal (Inevitable Defeat) and hold interaction for key threats. Stabilize with Soldier tokens and board wipes, then out-value the opponent with card draw and resilient threats. Do not overextend into sweepers or removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Stock Up and Jeskai Revelation to draw past their answers. Apply pressure with resilient threats and force them to spend interaction on your board. Avoid trading removal inefficiently; instead, force them to use multiple spells to deal with your threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war by prioritizing Jeskai Revelation to exile opponent's draw spells and recursion from the graveyard. Trade removal efficiently, favoring Inevitable Defeat on your own creatures to generate tokens and swing the board. Control the graveyard to deny their Jeskai Revelation value.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure control shell to a more reactive, hate-bear focused strategy. Add specific answers against top meta decks (e.g., graveyard hate for Landfall, counterspells or discard for Izzet). Adjust your curve by removing redundant draw spells and adding targeted interaction. Ensure your mana base can support the new card choices, and prioritize hands with early interaction and graveyard hate.
notes: Common mistakes include overusing Stock Up too early, ignoring graveyard recursion, and holding too much interaction until it's too late. Unique interaction: Inevitable Defeat on your own creature creates a 1/1 Soldier token, which can be used as a blocker, a sacrifice target, or a win condition. Jeskai Revelation specifically targets the opponent's graveyard, so use it to exile their recursion pieces and combo enablers. Always track opponent's graveyard to maximize Jeskai Revelation's value and avoid dying to unexpected recursion.

philosophy: Izzet Self-Bounce aims to disrupt the opponent while generating a relentless army of 1/1 tokens by repeatedly bouncing permanents and spells. It wins by out-value trading through tempo, leveraging bounce synergies to overwhelm control, combo, and midrange strategies before they can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one bounce spell (e.g., Unsummon, Return to the Ranks, Fog), and Flow State or Slickshot Show-Off. Prioritize hands that can play a land and cast a bounce spell or cheap threat on turn 1. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early interaction, or hands with multiple bounce spells but no mana/land base.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap bounce spell targeting an opponent's threat or your own creature to trigger Flow State. If you have Slickshot Show-Off, cast it on turn 2, bounce it back to hand, and cast it again if possible, or bounce a Flow State token to generate another. Turn 2: Continue casting bounce spells, prioritizing opponent's key permanents to disrupt their board while building your token army. Sequence lands to ensure consistent U/R mana.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono-Green Landfall, Izzet Prowess): Bring in board wipes (Fog, Wrath of God variants), more direct damage (Lightning Strike, Shock), and artifact/enchantment hate. Against control (Izzet Spells): Bring in more counterspell hate, card advantage (Thoughtseize/Discard if legal, or card draw), and additional bounce spells to out-value their interaction. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional disruption, and faster finishers. Against mirror: Bring in extra counterspells, more bounce spells, and cards that punish spell-heavy strategies.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve bounce spells for maximum impact—use them to trigger Flow State when you have multiple permanents on the board, or to remove opponent's key threats. Don't overcommit mana to bouncing your own tokens if you can stabilize first. Manage hand size by casting bounce spells as instants to respond to threats, and use Stormchaser's Talent to copy high-value bounce or removal spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough bounce spells to protect them or generate enough tokens to trade favorably. Hold back if the opponent has significant interaction (counters, removal) and you lack redundancy. Prioritize bouncing opponent's board wipes or key combo pieces over generating tokens if survival is at stake.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's interaction spells (counters, removal, board wipes) and adjust bounce targets accordingly. Track graveyard contents if they play graveyard-reliant decks. Note when they run out of interaction—this is your window to commit to an aggressive token push.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully developed Flow State board with multiple bounce spells in hand. Stormchaser's Talent copying a bounce spell to trigger Flow State twice in one turn. Slickshot Show-Off bouncing itself or other tokens to generate incremental value while applying pressure.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ Flow State triggers, enough bounce spells to protect your board, and the opponent has no board wipes or significant interaction. Also commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns with token damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single token with no bounce spells, the opponent has a board wipe or game-ending threat, and you have no way to rebuild. Also concede against matchups where your game plan is completely neutralized (e.g., heavy artifact/enchantment hate with no answers).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use bounce spells as instants to respond to counters, or cast them in response to opponent's spells to trigger Flow State before they resolve. Use Stormchaser's Talent to copy bounce spells after they've been countered, or use cheap threats to pressure through interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize bouncing their mana sources or key ramp creatures. Use Fog or similar effects to stall while you build your board. Don't trade tokens for their large creatures—focus on disruption and out-value.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use bounce spells to remove their early threats, then stabilize with Fog or similar effects. Once stabilized, transition to token generation and pressure. Prioritize survival over tempo in the first two turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early tokens and bounce spells to force them to use interaction. Use Stormchaser's Talent to copy their counterspells or removal to generate value. Out-value them by casting multiple bounce spells in a row to trigger Flow State repeatedly.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better card advantage and more efficient bounce spells. Use Stormchaser's Talent to copy opponent's bounce spells for extra triggers. Prioritize bouncing their Flow State or key creatures to disrupt their engine. Save interaction for their most valuable spells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your spell mix based on the metagame. Against spell-heavy decks, add more counterspells and discard to disrupt their hand. Against creature-heavy decks, add board wipes and targeted removal. Always ensure your sideboard includes at least one reliable way to stabilize against aggro and one way to close out control/combo games. Keep your bounce spell count consistent to maintain Flow State synergy.
notes: Common mistake: Bouncing your own tokens too early without a plan to protect them or generate value. Always calculate Flow State triggers—each bounce creates a token, so chaining bounces can snowball quickly. Be aware of 'bounce tax' (mana cost increases for repeated spells with the same name) and plan around it. Stormchaser's Talent can copy any instant or sorcery, so use it to copy bounce spells, removal, or even opponent's spells for value. Don't overextend into board wipes; always keep a bounce spell or Fog to reset the board if needed.

philosophy: Selesnya Gearhulk is a resilient midrange shell that leverages a dense artifact suite to fuel Gearhulk, creating explosive value swings by recursively key artifacts from the graveyard. It aims to out-value aggressive and combo decks through card advantage and board resilience, while grinding out control and mirror matches via superior resource generation and protected threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize fetchable duals or basics that enter untapped), plus at least one of Badgermole Cub, Ouroboroid, Keen-Eyed Curator, or Gearhulk. Keep hands with 3+ mana sources and a clear path to casting a 3-4 drop on turn 2 or 3. Mulligan pure curve decks, hands with only 1-2 lands, or hands lacking early artifacts/creatures to stabilize.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, ideally a fetch or basic that sets up W/G mana. Turn 2: Cast a 2-drop artifact or mana rock (e.g., Arcane Signet, Sol Ring, or early Cub/Ouroboroid if mana allows). Turn 3: Aim to cast Gearhulk or Keen-Eyed Curator to dig for more artifacts. Sequence lands to ensure double-colored mana for Gearhulk by turn 3-4, prioritizing untapped sources to maintain tempo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Spells: Bring in +1/+1 counters, efficient removal (e.g., Swords to Plowshares, Heroic Intervention), and artifact hate if needed. vs Control/Combo: Bring in more removal, graveyard hate, and late-game finishers. vs Mirror: Bring in artifact hate, additional counters, and extra Gearhulks to win the attrition war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping artifacts in the graveyard for Gearhulk recursion. Avoid overcommitting to the board if you lack protection or answers. Manage mana to ensure 4WWG is available for Gearhulk without flooding. Use Keen-Eyed Curator to dig for specific artifacts or Gearhulk itself, and track graveyard contents to maximize recursion value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or counters to protect them, or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back Gearhulk if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use artifacts as chump blockers or to stabilize against aggro, and only swing for damage when you can safely close the game or force unfavorable trades.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), artifact hate (e.g., Disenchant, Abrade), and graveyard hate. Note when opponents are holding up mana for counters or removal. Track graveyard contents to know what Gearhulk can return, and monitor opponent's hand size to gauge their ability to interact with your key threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Gearhulk returning a large artifact/creature, Keen-Eyed Curator finding a win-con, or a late-game artifact like Thran Dynamo combined with a big creature. The goal is to stabilize and then swing with protected threats or combo off with artifact synergy.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent is low on removal, or you can protect your board with counters/removal. Commit when you can safely cast Gearhulk and get a free value engine, or when you can force the opponent to use multiple answers on a single threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be interrupted, if you're completely locked out by hate cards, or if you're down to one card against a deck with multiple win conditions. Also concede if you're out of resources in a mirror and the opponent has more answers or better graveyard setup.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap artifacts to pressure, cast Gearhulk at end step or with mana open for protection. Cast multiple threats to force through, and use artifact spells that are hard to counter or cast in response to their counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with artifacts, use removal to stop key ramp pieces, and stabilize with board wipes or counters. Don't let them get too far ahead in mana, and prioritize disrupting their mana acceleration over trading creatures.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early artifacts/creatures to block, use removal on key threats, and stabilize with Gearhulk returning a large creature or artifact. Use counterspells/removal to control the board, and avoid overextending into sweepers or burn.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with artifacts, use Gearhulk to dig for answers or win-cons. Force them to use removal on multiple threats, and out-value them with card advantage. Play around sweepers by keeping threats in hand or using protection spells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better resource management, more answers, and better graveyard setup. Sideboard in more artifact hate and counters. Win the attrition war by protecting your Gearhulks, out-drawing them, and forcing unfavorable trades where you have more recursion options.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Against control, prioritize additional removal, graveyard hate, and late-game finishers. Against aggro/spells, bring in early interaction, +1/+1 counters, and artifact creatures to stabilize. In mirrors, focus on artifact hate, counters, and extra Gearhulks. Always maintain a balance of removal, counters, and finishers, and avoid over-indexing on one strategy.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board without protection. Gearhulk is highly vulnerable to sweepers, so always keep mana open for protection or recursion. Badgermole Cub and Ouroboroid are critical for fueling the engine and generating mana/boards. Keen-Eyed Curator is a powerful dig spell that can find Gearhulk or key artifacts. Don't forget to fetch lands for Gearhulk's mana cost, and track the graveyard carefully to maximize recursion value.

philosophy: Jeskai Control aims to neutralize early threats, out-value opponents through efficient card draw and targeted removal, and close out games with resilient threats or late-game card advantage engines. It excels against combo and midrange decks that rely on linear game plans, while struggling against highly aggressive or massively ramped strategies that can overwhelm its reactive toolkit.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetch/shock sources), a turn-1 discard spell, and a turn-2 interaction (counterspell, removal, or bounce). Mulligan to 5+ lands, pure removal without answers, or hands with multiple draw spells but no early interaction. Prioritize keeping lands that can fetch basics for Stock Up and Jeskai Revelation conditions.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize or Inquisition on the opponent's most threatening early card or key combo piece. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a counterspell or removal on the opponent's second-drop threat. If ahead, use Stock Up or Consult the Star Charts to dig for answers. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast colored spells on curve while maintaining land drops.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo: Bring in Lightning Helix, Rakdos Charm, Dovin's Veto, Swan Song, and artifact/enchantment hate. vs Control/Midrange: Bring in The Wandering Emperor, Karn, the Great Creator, Jeska's Thane, and additional counterspells. vs Mirror: Bring in Thoughtseize, Inquisition, Dovin's Veto, and artifact hate if needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for multiple interaction spells per turn; never overcommit to a single threat if you lack answers. Use Consult the Star Charts to find specific answers or finishers rather than randomizing. Manage graveyard by using Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void if needed, and avoid milling yourself with Jeskai Revelation unless you have a win condition or can discard non-essential cards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing your best threat until you have at least one interaction spell in hand. Trade efficiently: use removal on key threats, counterspells on game-ending spells, and discard on combo pieces. If the board is clear, develop your board with resilient threats like Teferi, Hero of Dominaria or Jeska's Thane.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana base (dual lands vs. shock lands vs. fetches) to predict their interaction. Track graveyard content for reanimation or storm threats. Note when they tap out for a big spell; that's your window to attack or cast a free interaction. Pay attention to discard targets to anticipate their hand.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Teferi, Hero of Dominaria (planeswalker loyalty plays), Jeska's Thane (attacking with creatures), The Wandering Emperor (combat tricks + card draw), or a well-timed Jeskai Revelation into a lethal threat. Secure the game by establishing a board presence that can't be easily wiped, then close with combat or a finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage locked, the opponent has no removal/counterspells in hand, or you've established a board that can trade favorably. Commit when you can safely cast Jeskai Revelation and still have a win condition in hand or on board.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards in hand with no interaction, facing a lethal board state with no way to clear it. Concede against highly linear combo decks if you've run out of answers and they have a guaranteed win condition. Also concede if you're consistently drawing dead lands or removal with no threats.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Cast threats at end of opponent's turn or in response to their spells. Use Consult the Star Charts to find uncounterable threats or protection. Employ cheap, efficient threats that can trade with their interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to disrupt mana acceleration. Use Thoughtseize/Inquisition on ramp spells. Bring in artifact/enchantment hate and board wipes. If they get ahead, use Teferi or Karn to stabilize and lock them out.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep early interaction (counters, removal, discard). Play aggressively with your own threats once stabilized. Use Swan Song or Dovin's Veto to protect your board. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and close the game quickly once you stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with superior card draw (Stock Up, Consult the Star Charts, Jeskai Revelation). Apply pressure with resilient threats they can't easily remove. Force them to use interaction on non-critical spells. Win the attrition war by having more answers and better late-game threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping hands with multiple interaction spells. Use Thoughtseize/Inquisition to strip their answers and force them to mulligan. Trade efficiently, save your best threat for when they're tapped out. Use Consult the Star Charts to find the exact answer they need.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift your game plan based on the metagame. Against faster decks, prioritize early interaction and clock. Against slower decks, bring in more card draw and resilient threats. Always carry at least one artifact/enchantment hate and one graveyard hate. Adjust your land count if needed, and ensure you have enough basic lands for Stock Up and Jeskai Revelation conditions.
notes: Jeskai Revelation is a double-edged sword; only cast it when you have a clear win condition or can discard non-essential cards. Consult the Star Charts is best used to find specific answers or finishers, not as a random draw spell. Watch for opponent's mana development; Jeskai's color fixing relies on fetch lands and shock lands, so they may be vulnerable to early pressure. Avoid overcommitting to the board without answers, as Jeskai Control is vulnerable to sweepers. Practice mulligan decisions carefully, as the deck's consistency hinges on early interaction and land sequencing.

philosophy: Golgari Midrange aims to stabilize early with efficient disruption and mana acceleration, then out-value opponents through superior card quality and graveyard synergy. It excels against fast, linear strategies by trading resources efficiently and closing out games with resilient, escalating threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early interaction (Deadly Cover-Up or discard), and a turn-2 or turn-3 threat. Mulligan on 2 lands with no early plays, 3+ lands with no threats or interaction, or hands with multiple copies of the same card that don't synergize with your midgame plan.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, potentially cast a cheap threat or hold for interaction. Turn 2: Ramp with Ancient Cornucopia or play a midgame threat like Professor Dellian Fel if mana allows. Prioritize establishing board presence while retaining removal for early aggression from Izzet or Landfall decks.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Landfall/Prowess): +1 more spot removal, +1 graveyard hate, -1 ramp. vs Control/Spells: +1 more discard, +1 late-game threat, -1 early interaction. vs Combo: +1 board wipe, +1 graveyard hate, -1 value engines. vs Mirror: +1 more efficient threats, +1 discard, -1 ramp.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for interaction vs. casting threats. Use Ancient Cornucopia to dig for consistency, but avoid sacrificing it prematurely if you lack follow-up. Leverage the graveyard for recursion or value, but track opponent graveyard hate to avoid overcommitting.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal to answer their early aggression. Hold back if you lack interaction and they have a wide board. Trade efficiently, prioritizing removal on key tempo or combo pieces over chump-blocking.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's tempo spells and Landfall triggers. Note when they tap out for big plays vs. holding up mana for counters/removal. Track graveyard contents for recursion threats and adjust your discard/removal priority accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Professor Dellian Fel as a resilient threat, combined with graveyard recursion or card advantage to overwhelm. Ancient Cornucopia can close games by drawing into the final answer or threat. Apply incremental pressure until they lack resources to respond.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, board control, and they have no immediate removal or board wipes. Go all-in if they're at low life and you can out-value their remaining resources. Commit when you've established a clear resource lead.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're consistently losing the resource war, have no answers to their win conditions, or are facing a locked-out combo/aggro board with no comeback potential. Fold when your hand is empty, your threats are removed, and they have a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats, discard to disrupt hand, and leverage graveyard value that can't be countered. Time spells around their counters and avoid overcommitting to single high-value plays.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Don't overcommit to board stalls; trade efficiently and transition to midgame threats before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and discard. Stabilize with efficient removal, then transition to midgame threats. Don't overextend into combat traps and always keep an answer for their finishers.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with resilient threats. Use discard to disrupt their hand. Out-value them with card advantage engines and force them to spend resources on removal instead of executing their game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize discard and efficient threats. Control the graveyard. Trade efficiently and out-value them in the late game. Sideboard in more interaction and late-game threats to tip the balance.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on matchup. Against faster decks, cut ramp and add more interaction/early threats. Against slower decks, add more late-game value and card draw. Always keep graveyard hate if recursion is prevalent, and consider cutting lands if you're consistently flooding.
notes: Avoid overcommitting to Ancient Cornucopia early if you lack protection. Professor Dellian Fel's value scales with board state, so protect it with removal or discard. Deadly Cover-Up is best used on key threats or to disrupt combo turns. Watch for format-specific hate cards and adjust your sideboard accordingly. Practice efficient mulligan decisions to avoid dead draws.

philosophy: 4c Kona is a spell-slinging Elemental engine that converts high spell density into a relentless stream of flying tokens via Kona, Emergent Elemental and Formidable Speaker. It aims to overwhelm fast, interactive, and combo-heavy archetypes by out-casting opponents and finishing with high-impact spells like Omniscience or Ashling, Rekindled.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (duals, fetches, or ramp like Sol Ring/Arcane Signet) plus at least one early spell engine (Kona, Formidable Speaker, or a cheap cantrip/ramp). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only lands, or a hand of high-cost finishers without early outlets. Prioritize keeping hands that can cast 2+ spells by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a mana accelerator or cantrip. Turn 2: Ramp into Kona or Formidable Speaker, or cast a cheap Elemental to start the token pipeline. Sequence lands to ensure 3-4 mana by turn 3. If Omniscience is in hand, hold it until you can cast a high-value spell for free, then drop it to swing for lethal or establish board dominance.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono-Green Landfall, Izzet Prowess): Bring in early removal, counterspells, and spot interaction. Against control/spells: Bring in more spell acceleration, card draw, and direct damage. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional counters, and hand disruption. Against mirror: Bring in efficient removal, counters, and card advantage to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource for spell density, not just for casting Kona. Keep 1-2 lands in hand if drawing multiple spells. Avoid self-mill unless it fuels a specific win condition. Use cantrips to maintain hand size and continuously trigger Kona/Formidable Speaker.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or counterspells to protect them, or when you can out-value the opponent's board. Hold back if facing heavy board wipes or targeted removal; instead, focus on card advantage and setting up Omniscience. Don't overcommit to a single token if you can stabilize with spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Cyclonic Rift, Wrath of God), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Strike), and counterspells. Note when opponents are holding up mana for interaction vs. casting spells. Track their graveyard if they are playing combo or reanimation strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Omniscience + Ashling, Rekindled or Kona/Formidable Speaker is the primary win condition. Cast Omniscience, then cast Ashling or multiple Elementals for free to flood the board with flying tokens. Alternatively, use a high-impact spell or big Elemental finisher to close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Omniscience in play, enough mana to cast multiple spells, and the opponent has no immediate board wipe or removal. Also commit when you can out-value their remaining life total with token damage and have no reason to hold back interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if facing a hard-lock combo you cannot disrupt, if you are down to 1-2 cards with no way to cast spells or generate tokens, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board wipe/removal lock. Also concede if you are out of resources in a mirror and they have more card advantage.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target spells or cast instants in response to their counters to trigger Kona/Formidable Speaker. Use spell acceleration to cast multiple spells in a single turn, forcing them to counter multiple targets. Prioritize uncounterable effects or spell-skew if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap Elementals and cantrips. Use removal to hit their ramp spells or mana rocks. Do not let them establish a massive mana advantage; disrupt their early game with interaction and keep the board state fluid.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early removal and counterspells. Use Kona/Formidable Speaker to generate flying tokens that can block or trade efficiently. Stabilize with card draw and hold up interaction until they commit to attacks.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with spell density and card advantage. Use cantrips to dig for key pieces. Pressure their life total with flying tokens while they are stuck on land drops or board wipes. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw, removal, and counterspells. Control the board with Kona/Formidable Speaker, but be ready to trade efficiently. Use hand disruption to break their rhythm and win by out-resourcing and out-answering.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw spell density to targeted interaction. Against fast decks, prioritize early removal and counters. Against control/combo, bring in more card advantage and disruption. In mirrors, focus on hand disruption and efficient removal. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards, and adjust your play order to account for sideboard changes.
notes: Kona and Formidable Speaker trigger on any spell you cast, including instants and activated abilities that count as spells. Cast instants at optimal times to maximize token generation. Omniscience is fragile; protect it with counterspells or sacrifice outlets if needed. Common mistake: overcommitting to Kona without follow-up spells. Always keep a hand of spells to fuel the engine. Ashling, Rekindled's death trigger creates a 2/2, which can be sacrificed for value or used to trade with removal.

philosophy: Bant Ouroboroid is a resilient midrange/control archetype that leverages recursive value engines and flexible interaction to outlast faster, resource-intensive strategies. It aims to stabilize early, grind out card advantage through its signature recursion loop, and close games with resilient threats or incremental damage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a playable land (fetch/shock/basic) and a turn-1 or turn-2 threat or interaction. Prioritize hands with 2-3 lands and at least one of Badgermole Cub, Quantum Riddler, or Nature's Rhythm. Mulligan pure curve-outs, triple-land hands, or hands lacking early interaction against Izzet spells/landfall. Keep hands with 3+ lands if they include a turn-2 play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap interaction or hold for a threat. Turn 2: Deploy Badgermole Cub or Nature's Rhythm if mana allows, or play a land and hold up interaction. Sequence lands to ensure W/U/G availability by turn 3. If facing aggro, prioritize cheap removal/counters over developing the board. If facing control, establish a threat and hold up counterspells for key spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in artifact hate, additional counterspells, and card draw to out-grind their tempo. Against Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in sweepers, early removal, and graveyard hate if recursion is targeted. Against control/mirror: Bring in more value engines, card advantage, and targeted removal. Against combo: Bring in specific hate, additional counters, and lifegain.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for flexible interaction until threats are committed. Use the graveyard strategically for recursion but avoid overcommitting to a single engine. Manage hand size by trading interaction for card advantage when ahead.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have answer coverage or when the board state justifies the risk. Hold back against sweepers or mass removal. Prioritize trading low-value creatures for high-value threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet players holding up counterspells on your key engines; bait them with cheap spells first. Monitor Landfall decks for their land drops and trigger counts. Track graveyard size for recursion threats. Note when opponents start tapping out for big spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Close games with resilient threats that can trade into removal, or by chaining recursion engines to generate incremental damage/card advantage. Use Nature's Rhythm to fuel spells or draw into game-ending threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, board presence, and sufficient interaction to handle their response. Commit when the opponent is at low life or has no answers to your key threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you're consistently one-shot, have no interaction against their win conditions, or are facing a dead hand with no comeback potential. Concede if you're out of resources against a locked board or unstoppable combo.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Bait counters with cheap spells, use split-second or uncounterable threats, and leverage recursion to out-value their interaction. Play around their main counters by holding up protection or using layered spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their land drops, use efficient removal on key ramp creatures, and stabilize with sweepers or lifegain. Out-grind them with card advantage once they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and cheap removal to stabilize. Use Nature's Rhythm to draw into answers or threats. Transition to midrange control once stabilized, then close with resilient threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their life total with multiple threats, force them to use interaction on low-value targets, and leverage card advantage to out-resource them. Use recursion to out-value their removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by playing more efficiently, holding up interaction for their key engines, and leveraging card advantage. Trade up on creatures, use recursion to out-value their engines, and force them to make mistakes in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction suite based on the metagame. Against tempo/control, increase counterspells and card draw. Against aggro/ramp, prioritize early removal and sweepers. In mirrors, focus on card advantage and targeted hate. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards, and adjust your mulligan strategy accordingly.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without answer coverage, mismanaging mana for flexible interaction, and failing to track opponent's graveyard or counterspells. Unique interactions include using Nature's Rhythm to fuel recursion loops and leveraging Quantum Riddler to disrupt opponent's spell sequencing. Always prioritize game plan consistency over flashy plays.

philosophy: Azorius Tempo aims to out-value opponents through efficient interaction and card selection, trading up on resources while stabilizing the board. It wins by out-stalling aggressive, spell-heavy, and ramp strategies, then closing out games with resilient threats or game-swinging sweepers once the early game is controlled.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/fixing), at least one early interaction (Aven Interrupter, Voice of Victory, or a cheap counter/removal), and a curve-appropriate threat. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of expensive spells, or no early interaction if the metagame is fast. Keep hands that can play a turn-1 Aven Interrupter or a turn-2 threat/interaction combo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Aven Interrupter if available, or hold up mana for a turn-1 interaction if the opponent is known to play vulnerable early threats. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a 2-drop threat or interaction. Prioritize playing lands to ensure you hit key mana steps (3, 4, 5). If drawing Aven Interrupter on turn 1, consider holding up mana to protect it or answer a key spell, as its ward/counter ability can swing early trades.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in additional counterspells, Voice of Victory copies, and artifact/enchantment hate. vs Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in Avatar's Wrath copies, additional efficient removal, and life gain. vs Control/Mirror: Bring in card advantage engines, additional late-game threats, and hate for specific combo or recursion pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve interaction for high-value targets (key threats, combo pieces, or ramp accelerators). Avoid overcommitting to clearing minor threats if you can out-value them. Manage hand size by playing threats that can be answered, then recasting or drawing into more. Graveyard is rarely a primary resource, but monitor opponent recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent has no answers. Hold back if you suspect a board wipe or heavy removal. Prioritize trading up: use Aven Interrupter's ward to protect a key threat or kill a vulnerable one. Never overextend into a known sweepers without a plan to rebuild.
