threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use better sequencing, sideboard cards, and card advantage. Prioritize keeping interaction to counter their key spells. Win by out-drawing and out-resolving. Track their hand size and available interaction to time your plays.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a control-oriented game plan to a more aggressive or specialized one depending on the matchup. Against aggro, prioritize early interaction and cheap threats. Against control/combo, bring in more counters, card draw, and finishers. In the mirror, focus on sideboard cards that disrupt synergies and provide card advantage. Always keep Roaming Throne if you have enough cheap spells to copy, and adjust your land base if the format requires specific colors for sideboard cards.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board without protection or tracking opponent's interaction. Unique interaction: Roaming Throne can copy your own counterspells to protect multiple threats, or copy removal to clear multiple blockers. Wistfulness dies to sacrifice outlets or combat, so plan trades carefully. Watch for format-specific hate cards like graveyard hate or mass removal. Always track opponent's hand size and available interaction to time your plays effectively.

philosophy: Sultai Control aims to out-value opponents through efficient disruption, card advantage, and graveyard recursion, ultimately closing out games against midrange and combo decks while weathering early aggression. It thrives in attrition, leveraging Flow State to replay key spells and Ancient Cornucopia to sustain resource advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including duals or mana rocks), at least one early interaction (Rakshasa's Bargain, discard, or cheap removal), and a Flow State or Ancient Cornucopia if possible. Mulligan to hands with only one land, no early answers, or excessive high-cost spells. Prioritize keeping hands that can stabilize turn 1-2 while setting up the late-game engine.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast Rakshasa's Bargain to disrupt an opponent's key play or draw into a better hand. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, cast Ancient Cornucopia for early card advantage/mana, or play efficient removal/threats. Sequence lands to ensure blue/black/green availability by turn 3 for key spells like counterspells or discard.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess, Mono-Green Landfall): Bring in direct damage, counterspells, and early interaction; cut some expensive finishers. vs Control/Combo (Izzet Spells, Spellementals): Bring in graveyard hate, additional counters, and card draw; keep Flow State recursion. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and hand disruption; prioritize card advantage tools.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 4-5, then commit to board development. Use Rakshasa's Bargain efficiently to maintain hand size without overcommitting. Leverage Ancient Cornucopia's sacrifice for card draw when hand is full or mana is saturated. Protect Flow State in the graveyard; it is your primary win condition engine.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing all threats until you have a clear answer to the opponent's primary win condition. Use removal efficiently on key threats, saving counterspells for game-ending plays. Commit when you have graveyard recursion online or a clear path to closing out the game.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent mana development (especially Izzet's ramp/mana rocks) and graveyard activity. Note when they start casting high-cost spells or combo pieces. Track their hand size after Rakshasa's Bargain or discard effects to anticipate their next play.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Flow State recursion engine, often paired with Phyrexian Unlife or a combo finisher, plus Ancient Cornucopia for infinite card draw/mana in some configurations. Close the game by replaying high-impact spells or combo pieces from the graveyard.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Flow State online, sufficient mana to replay key spells, and no immediate threat to your life total. Commit when the opponent has no answers to your recursion or when you can stabilize and out-value them.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 1-2 cards, have no interaction, and the opponent is on a clear combo or aggressive path with no way to stabilize. Concede if your key recursion engine is destroyed and you lack alternative win conditions.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost spells to force counters, then resolve key threats. Employ flashback/graveyard recursion to bypass counters. Use Ancient Cornucopia to dig for answers or combo pieces.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to disrupt mana development. Use counterspells and discard to slow them down. Leverage Ancient Cornucopia for card advantage to out-value their acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal, counters, and Rakshasa's Bargain to disrupt their hand. Play efficiently to survive turns 1-3, then transition to control and close with recursion.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with mid-game threats and hand disruption. Use Flow State to out-value their removal. Dig deeper with Ancient Cornucopia to find answers or finishers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, efficient removal, and graveyard protection. Use Rakshasa's Bargain to out-draw them. Protect Flow State and disrupt their recursion.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the metagame. Against faster decks, increase early interaction and reduce expensive finishers. Against control/combo, add graveyard hate and counterspells. Always keep Flow State and Ancient Cornucopia as core recursion/advantage tools. Adjust land counts if facing heavy mana denial or aggressive strategies.
notes: Flow State is fragile; protect it from discard and exile effects. Ancient Cornucopia's sacrifice timing is crucial—do not sacrifice prematurely. Rakshasa's Bargain is best used to disrupt key turns or refill hand when low. Avoid overcommitting to the board without recursion online. Watch for opponent sideboard cards like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void.

philosophy: Izzet Control aims to stabilize the early game with efficient interaction and card advantage, then out-value opponents through superior resource management and late-game threats. It excels against combo and midrange decks that rely on committing resources early, while struggling against highly aggressive or fast-ramp strategies that can overwhelm its defensive suite.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with two lands (preferably one fetchable or dual) and at least one removal spell (Ashling's Command) or a turn-1/2 play. Keep hands with Tablet of Discovery if paired with a land and a cantrip/removal. Mulligan anything with three+ lands, three+ non-basic lands without fetches, or hands lacking early interaction against aggro/ramp.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cantrip or hold up interaction. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Tablet of Discovery for mana acceleration and card draw, or play a cheap threat/interaction. Prioritize maintaining open mana for counterspells/removal through opponent's turns.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono-Green Landfall, Izzet Prowess): Bring in additional direct damage, discard, or early board wipes. Remove heavy late-game finishers. vs Control/Spells (Izzet Spells, Izzet Spellementals): Bring in card advantage engines, counterspell upgrades, and threat protection. vs Ramp (Selesnya Landfall): Bring in artifact/enchantment hate, additional removal, and early pressure. vs Mirror: Bring in additional counterspells, card draw, and threat protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until the opponent commits. Use Tablet of Discovery to cycle into answers or threats. Never overextend into known removal; keep a hand of 3-4 cards to navigate the late game. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary sacrifice outlets unless necessary, as Ashling's Command can exile threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have answered or out-value the opponent's board. Use Ashling's Command to remove key permanents while gaining life or drawing cards. Hold back on casting expensive finishers until you've established card advantage or removed the opponent's primary answer.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana bases and land drops to predict ramp or combo turns. Track opponent's hand size and graveyard to anticipate combo pieces or value engines. Note when they hold up mana past turn 3, indicating potential counterspells or late-game threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Colorstorm Stallion serves as the primary win condition, often supported by Ashling's Command for protection or removal. Tablet of Discovery ensures you draw into your finisher and have mana to cast it. Chain cantrips and interaction to stabilize, then deploy the Stallion when the board is clear or protected.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no answers in hand, or you've removed their primary interaction. Commit when you can safely cast Colorstorm Stallion and have backup interaction to protect it or close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, have no cards in hand, and the opponent has a lethal threat or combo piece ready. Concede against fast aggro if your early interaction is exhausted and you lack board wipes. Fold if you're locked out of your game plan by hate cards and have no sideboard answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ashling's Command to remove counterspellers or protect your spells. Cast threats in response to their counterspells to force trades. Use Tablet of Discovery to dig for alternative paths or additional interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early removal and discard to disrupt their mana development. Use Tablet of Discovery to race their acceleration while maintaining card advantage. Bring in artifact/enchantment hate and early board control.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep a wide hand of removal and cantrips. Use Ashling's Command to clear early threats and stabilize. Play Tablet of Discovery to draw into board wipes or additional answers. Avoid overcommitting to threats until you've stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage and efficient threats. Use Ashling's Command to remove their key permanents. Pressure with Colorstorm Stallion when they lack interaction. Win the resource war by cycling faster and applying incremental pressure.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Use Tablet of Discovery to out-draw them. Protect your threats with Ashling's Command. Hold up counterspells for their key plays. Force them to overcommit by threatening with Colorstorm Stallion when they've exhausted their interaction.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a reactive control shell to a more adaptive game plan. Against aggressive decks, prioritize early interaction and board wipes. Against control and combo, increase card advantage and threat protection. Always carry hate cards for the top metagame archetypes, and adjust your land base if needed to support specific colors or utility lands.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to threats without answering the board, mismanaging open mana for interaction, and failing to track opponent's graveyard/hand size. Tablet of Discovery is a double-edged sword; use it to dig for answers early, but be mindful of flooding your board with non-creatures. Ashling's Command's modes should be chosen based on the board state: exile for key threats, draw for card advantage, or life gain for stabilization. Colorstorm Stallion requires protection or a clear board to be effective; don't cast it into known removal.

philosophy: Mono-Blue Spellementals is a spell-slinging tempo and value engine that wins by casting a high volume of cheap spells to fuel its Spellemental mechanics, leveraging card advantage and disruption to out-resource opponents. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on singular big plays, but must navigate fast aggro and heavy ramp carefully.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (or 1 land if on the play) and at least one 1-2 mana spell or key engine card like Eddymurk Crab or Three Steps Ahead. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early spells, multiple high-cost spells, or no way to interact with the board.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-mana cantrip or cheap spell to start the spell count. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-mana spell or deploy Eddymurk Crab. Prioritize casting spells over attacking to build engine value. If you have Three Steps Ahead, cast it early to dig for more spells or protect your board state.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in counterspells, spot removal, and early blockers. vs Control: Bring in additional cantrips, card draw, and direct damage. vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, artifact/enchantment removal, and extra counters. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, spell acceleration, and disruption to break their spell count.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction and spell casting; avoid overcommitting to the board without protection. Use cantrips to maintain hand size and ensure you always have spells to cast. If the format utilizes the graveyard, protect it or leverage it for value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the board is clear. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Prioritize casting spells over attacking early to keep the Spellemental engine running.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and targeted removal. Note when opponents hold up mana for interaction. Track their graveyard if relevant, and monitor for combo pieces or win conditions that could end the game unexpectedly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sunderflock as a late-game threat or combo piece, or a high-spell-count win condition. Use Three Steps Ahead to dig for the finisher or protection when needed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough spell count to make threats unblockable or unkillable, or when the opponent has exhausted their interaction. Commit when you can safely attack or cast your win condition without fear of a sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one card, the opponent has a board wipe ready, and you have no way to cast spells or remove their threats. Concede in inherently unfavorable matchups where you lack the tools to stabilize or out-value them.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast spells in response to counters, use cheap spells to overwhelm counters, or employ protection. Split your threats across multiple turns to reduce the impact of single-target counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use early interaction, discard, or counters to stop their big spells. Leverage cantrips to stay ahead in cards and pressure before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early blockers, counters, and spot removal. Do not overcommit to casting spells if you need to block. Stabilize first, then resume your spell-slinging game plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap threats and use cantrips to dig through their removal. Win with value or combo before they can stabilize. Avoid trading into their removal unnecessarily.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the spell war by prioritizing card advantage, protecting your engine, and disrupting their spell count. Sideboard targeted removal and counters to break their momentum.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction based on the metagame. Against spell-heavy formats, bring in more counters and protection. Against aggressive formats, bring in early interaction and blockers. Always keep your core engine intact and adjust your mulligan strategy to reflect the new card composition.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to attacking instead of casting spells, which stalls your engine. Unique interaction: Three Steps Ahead can be used to find protection or finishers, while Eddymurk Crab provides early value or spell acceleration. Sunderflock is your late-game win condition. Play around sweepers and counters, and always prioritize maintaining spell density in your deck.

philosophy: Survive the early game with flexible interaction and card advantage, then close out games with late-game finishers like Jeskai Revelation. It excels against combo and aggressive strategies that run out of resources, but requires careful sequencing to beat resilient ramp or spell-heavy decks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fixing), at least one early interaction (removal/counterspell) or draw spell (Stock Up), and a clear win condition or path to it. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands full of late-game cards with zero interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land, pass. Turn 2: Play a dual/fixing land, cast a cheap interaction or Stock Up to stabilize. Prioritize maintaining card parity and board control over developing a board. Against aggro, prioritize cheap removal/counters over digging.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Control: Bring in additional counterspells, card advantage, and artifact/enchantment hate. Against Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in more board wipes (Day of Judgment), creature removal, and land/ramp hate. Against Izzet Prowess/Spellementals: Bring in cheap counters, spot removal, and life gain. Against mirror: Bring in additional card advantage and finishers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource for answers, not just casting threats. Keep 1-2 lands untapped for instant-speed interaction. Manage hand size by trading answers for threats when ahead, and dig with Stock Up when behind or facing lock pieces. Avoid overcommitting to the board; control wins on card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to resolution or when the opponent has no interaction left. Hold back on playing Jeskai Revelation until you can safely exile a win condition or have protection. Use board wipes when the opponent has 2+ threats or when you're at low life.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspell stacks (Izzet decks), land acceleration (Landfall decks), and spell chains (Prowess/Spellementals). Key tells: Opponent tapping for multiple colors early, leaving mana open on turns 2-3, or discarding/drawing into specific combo pieces. Track graveyard hate needs against discard or mill strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Jeskai Revelation is the primary win condition; cast it when you can safely exile a game-winning spell or creature. Secondary finishers include late-game creatures or direct damage spells if available.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no interaction, or you've established board control and can safely cast your finisher. Also commit when you're at low life and need to race or close the game before they stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're consistently one-shot, have no interaction or win conditions, or are facing a combo deck that has assembled its win condition and you lack the specific hate. Also concede if you're out of cards and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats to force counters, then resolve your finisher. Utilize flashback, exile effects, or uncounterable spells if available. Dig deeper to find answers to their counters or alternative win conditions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction and board wipes. Don't let them get ahead on mana; use cheap removal and counters to disrupt their land drops. If they stabilize, out-value them with card advantage and late-game threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play aggressively with interaction, hold up counterspells/removal for key threats, and stabilize with board wipes. Use life gain if available. Don't overextend; control the board until you can close the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Trade resources efficiently, use card advantage to out-draw them, and pressure with threats they can't answer. Force them to use their interaction on low-impact spells, then resolve your finisher.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win on card advantage and better sequencing. Use Stock Up to dig deeper, hold up interaction to counter their key spells, and close the game with Jeskai Revelation or a superior finisher. Track their hand size and graveyard.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against spell-heavy decks, increase counterspells and card draw. Against creature/ramp decks, add more board wipes and spot removal. Remove redundant cards that don't address the specific matchup. Maintain a balance between interaction and win conditions, and ensure your mana base can support the sideboarded cards.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board too early, failing to hold up mana for interaction, and mismanaging Jeskai Revelation's exile trigger. Always track the opponent's graveyard and hand size. In unknown formats, adapt quickly to new threats by leveraging 5c's flexibility. Remember that control wins by making better decisions, not by having the biggest threats.

philosophy: Rakdos Monument is a resilient midrange deck that leverages artifact value, discard, and direct damage to weather early aggression, stabilize the board, and close out games with a massively empowered Monument or overwhelming late-game threats. It excels against fast, spell-heavy, or ramp-based archetypes by trading early tempo for late-game inevitability and card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a turn-1 or turn-2 discard/removal spell, and at least one early threat or mana accelerator. Prioritize hands that can play a land, cast a cheap interaction, and develop toward the Monument by turn 3. Mulligan any hand with 0-1 lands, multiple expensive cards with no early play, or only removal without threats or mana fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap discard or early threat if available. Turn 2: Develop mana, drop the Monument if you have the mana, or establish a midrange threat. Prioritize board presence and card advantage over rushing; the Monument needs time to grow and stabilize against the fast metagame.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in extra discard, artifact hate, and cheap sweepers (e.g., Pyroclasm-style effects) to handle spell-slinging and counters. vs Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in early removal, sweepers, and graveyard hate if recursion is present. vs Control/Mirror: Bring in more resilient threats, card advantage engines, and protection spells to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for interaction versus Monument development. Avoid overcommitting to the artifact early; keep mana open for disruption and protection. Manage your graveyard carefully if the deck uses it for recursion or value, and prioritize drawing into late-game threats when the board is stabilized.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or discard to clear blockers or answer sweepers. Hold back if the opponent has heavy removal or counterspells and you lack protection. The Monument is a primary win condition but also a target; protect it with discard or hold it back until you can safely attack.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's counterspells—hold up mana for protection or cast key spells at instant speed when they're tapped out. Track Landfall triggers and wide boards; have sweepers ready before they stabilize. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or sweepers; this is your window to attack or drop the Monument safely.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully grown Monument-boosted creature, often paired with direct damage or a resilient threat like Moonshadow. Sometimes a sacrifice outlet combined with recursion or evasion can close games. Direct damage spells can also finish games if the opponent is low and your board is established.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent is low on removal, or you've established board control. Commit aggressively if the Monument is untouchable or if you can trade efficiently into their blockers. Push damage when you know they lack sweepers or counters.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no interaction or if you're down to 1-2 cards with no way to break through. Also concede against fast combo or Landfall if you cannot stabilize by turn 4-5 and have no comeback threats. Don't overextend into known sweepers or counterspells.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats or discard to remove counterspells before they can be cast. Cast key spells at instant speed when the opponent is tapped out. Rely on the Monument as a secondary win condition that doesn't require casting spells, and use discard to disrupt their hand before they can answer your threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development and use discard to hit key ramp pieces. Stabilize with sweepers and early removal, then close out the game before they hit their late-game power. Don't let them stabilize; trade efficiently and pressure their life total.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early removal and discard to slow their board development. Don't overextend; the Monument wins long games, so survive turns 1-3, stabilize, and out-value them. Use direct damage to trade down favorably and protect your life total while developing your board.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their life total with direct damage and resilient threats. Force them to use removal on the Monument, then exploit the card advantage. Use discard to disrupt their game plan and force unfavorable trades. Don't let them stabilize; keep applying pressure and win the resource war.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing card advantage, protecting your Monument, and forcing unfavorable trades. Discard their key threats and out-value them in the late game. Track their removal and counters, and play around their sideboard by keeping a balanced hand of interaction and threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific metagame threats. Against spell-heavy decks, add more discard and artifact hate. Against aggressive or ramp decks, add early removal and life gain if available. Always maintain a balance between interaction and threats; avoid over-indexing on one side. Keep a flexible game plan that can adapt to midrange, control, or combo strategies.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the Monument too early and getting wiped by sweepers or targeted removal. Another pitfall: Ignoring the graveyard if the deck uses it for recursion or value. Unique interaction: Moonshadow likely synergizes with the Monument through recursion, evasion, or sacrifice outlets. Always track opponent's mana and removal to time your key plays, and don't be afraid to play around sweepers by holding up interaction or attacking in waves.

philosophy: Sultai Ouroboroid is a recursive midrange archetype that leverages land synergy and self-referential value engines to out-grind opponents. It aims to stabilize against fast aggro and spell-heavy strategies by efficiently trading resources, then closes out games with resilient threats or looping combos that out-value control and landfall decks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Llanowar Elves, Nature's Rhythm, or early interactive threats. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple high-cost finishers without ramp, or hands lacking early interaction. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a Llanowar Elves on turn one, or a land and Nature's Rhythm on turn two.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, tap Llanowar Elves if possible. Turn 2: Ramp into Nature's Rhythm or deploy a 2-drop threat. Sequence lands to ensure Sultai colors by turn 2-3, prioritizing fetches/shocks if available. Use Nature's Rhythm to draw cards while slowing opponent's land drops, setting up a stable mana base for midgame recursion.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet/Spells: Bring in countermagic, artifact hate, and cheap removal. vs Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in graveyard hate, enchantment removal, and board wipes. vs Aggro: Bring in early interaction, life gain, and efficient spot removal. vs Mirror: Bring in additional recursion, card draw, and targeted removal to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 3. Use the graveyard strategically; avoid over-committing to recursion if the board is unstable. Manage hand size by trading cards for board presence, and use Nature's Rhythm to cycle into answers or threats when resources are tight.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready or the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if facing sweepers or heavy removal. Prioritize trading into key threats before developing your own board, and use Badgermole Cub's recursion to maintain pressure without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's counterspells and cheap removal to time your plays around them. Note when Landfall decks start dropping multiple lands per turn to anticipate their power spike. Track graveyard contents for recursion threats or combo pieces, and adjust your removal priorities accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Deploy resilient threats that can't be easily removed, or chain recursion engines to generate lethal damage or card advantage. Use late-game Nature's Rhythm to draw into win conditions or finish off low-life opponents with recursive threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, the opponent is low on removal, or you've established a recursive loop that can't be answered. Commit when you can force unfavorable trades or when your opponent is tapped out for interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold against locked-out control with no draw spells, or against aggro with no early interaction and low life. Concede if your recursion engine is permanently removed and you have no alternative win conditions, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split spells, cheap threats, or layered effects to force counterspells. Trade resources to burn through their protection, then play around the last one. Use graveyard recursion to bypass counters and apply pressure from unexpected angles.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Disrupt their mana development with land hate or counters, and avoid trading into their late-game finishers. Stabilize quickly and close out before they reach their power spike.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal and life gain. Don't over-commit to big threats; trade efficiently and close out quickly once stabilized. Use recursion to maintain card advantage and outlast their early burst.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and use card advantage to out-value them. Exploit their lack of early interaction and force them to use resources inefficiently. Chain recursion to generate value they can't answer with single-target removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing card draw, recursion, and targeted removal. Control the graveyard and force unfavorable trades. Out-value them in the late game by maintaining a steady stream of threats and answers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate and adjust your curve. Against spell-heavy decks, prioritize counters and artifact hate. Against landfall, bring graveyard hate and enchantment removal. Remove redundant early threats and add late-game finishers or interaction as needed to smooth your game plan.
notes: Common mistake: Over-ramping into a single win condition. Always maintain a board presence and keep interactive spells in hand. Unique interaction: Nature's Rhythm can be used to tutor for lands or trigger landfall synergies. Badgermole Cub's recursion can be exploited by graveyard hate, so protect it or have backup plans. Practice timing your recursion to avoid giving opponents free interaction windows.

philosophy: This deck aims to stabilize the early game with efficient interaction and resilient threats, then out-value opponents through card advantage and incremental board development. It excels in longer games against spell-heavy and combo archetypes while grinding out aggressive matchups with timely removal and pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one turn-1 or turn-2 interaction (Requiting Hex, early removal), and a 2-drop threat or midrange engine. Mulligan pure curve toppers, triple lands, or hands lacking early answers against spell-heavy matchups.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land; drop Requiting Hex or cheap removal if opponent plays a problematic early spell, otherwise develop mana. Turn 2: Play a 2-drop threat or interaction depending on board state. Prioritize lands that enter untapped or fetch sources if available; otherwise, sequence basics to ensure consistent black mana for threats and interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in additional counters, discard, and artifact/enchantment hate. Against Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in efficient removal, life gain (if applicable), and early pressure. Against Control: Bring in more threats, card draw, and direct damage. Against Mirror: Bring in targeted hate, additional interaction, and card advantage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between interaction and threat development; avoid flooding by holding lands if you have sufficient answers. Manage hand size to maintain card advantage, and use the graveyard strategically if the deck includes recursion or sacrifice outlets.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to clear blockers or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if facing sweepers, heavy counters, or if you need to preserve mana for protection. Trade efficiently to maintain board presence without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or leave mana open for interaction. Track graveyard usage and life totals to time your finishers correctly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Bloodletter of Aclazotz generating deathtouch tokens, Unstoppable Slasher with accumulated +1/+1 counters, or a well-timed Requiting Hex to protect a win condition or remove a key blocker. Chain threats to overwhelm interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no interaction, is low on life, or you hold a decisive card advantage. Commit when you can force through multiple threats or when your board state is resilient enough to absorb a single answer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold against locked-out control with no removal, unanswerable combo wins, or if you are consistently losing the race to aggro with no stabilization tools. Concede if you are dead-drawing into irrelevant cards while the opponent is one step from victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Requiting Hex to counter their counters or cast threats in response to their spells. Apply multiple threats to force through, and consider sacrificing or discarding to bait counters before committing your finisher.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use interaction to disrupt their mana development. Don't get outpaced; stabilize quickly with removal and transition to midrange threats before they can deploy their win conditions.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize efficient removal and life management. Stabilize with midrange threats that can trade favorably, then close out the game with incremental pressure. Avoid overcommitting to the board before you have card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure to force them to use interaction on threats rather than combo pieces. Leverage card advantage to out-grind them, and use Requiting Hex to protect your win conditions or remove key control pieces.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by sequencing better, sideboarding in targeted hate, and leveraging card advantage. Force them to use interaction inefficiently, and prioritize board presence over single threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your deck to the specific metagame. Increase interaction and hate against spell-heavy and combo decks, prioritize removal and life against aggro, and bring in more threats and card draw against control. In mirrors, sideboard in additional interaction and targeted hate to win the resource war. Always track opponent tendencies and adjust your sideboard strategy accordingly.
notes: Avoid overcommitting to a single threat without backup. Practice timing Requiting Hex to maximize its dual effect. Watch for format-specific rules that may affect land sequencing or card legality. Common mistake: holding too much mana for interaction and getting overrun; balance aggression and defense. If the deck includes graveyard recursion, manage it carefully to avoid giving opponents free targets.

philosophy: Temur Kona is a spell-synergy combo-control deck that aims to cast a high volume of spells, leveraging Omniscience to bypass mana constraints and trigger spell-based synergies. It excels against spell-heavy and landfall archetypes by out-resourcing their triggers and closing out games with high-impact threats like North Wind Avatar.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetches/shocks if in format), at least one early spell or ramp piece, and a clear path to casting Formidable Speaker or Omniscience by turn 3-4. Mulligan if you have zero lands, only late-game threats without enablers, or a hand entirely composed of high-cost spells with no early interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap cantrip or ramp spell to dig for your enablers. Turn 2: Develop mana base, cast additional spells to build synergy counters/triggers, and set up for Omniscience. Land sequencing: Prioritize sources that enter untapped or fetch lands to smooth draws; avoid clumping colors early.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals: Bring in counterspells, spell-slow, and graveyard hate to disrupt their stack and recursion. Against Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in board wipes, artifact/enchantment removal, and targeted creature removal to break their engine. Against Aggro/Control: Prioritize early interaction and spot removal to stabilize before going off.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard low-cost spells to fuel spell triggers and ensure you have enough resources to cast Omniscience and follow-up threats. Manage mana carefully to avoid flooding; use cantrips to dig for answers. Treat the graveyard as a secondary resource for recursion or spell synergy, but protect it from hate if the format rewards it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to resolution or can protect them with counterspells/removal. Hold back high-value spells if the board is unstable or if you're facing a hard counter to your game plan. Use removal efficiently to clear key threats without overcommitting mana.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's counterspells and stack manipulation; note when they hold up mana for removal versus counters. Track landfall triggers on Selesnya/Mono-Green and anticipate their big turns. Pay attention to graveyard activity and hand size to gauge their combo potential.

endgame_tactics.finishers: North Wind Avatar (high power/flying finisher) and Formidable Speaker (spell-synergy enabler/finisher). Win by casting these with Omniscience or building enough spell triggers to overwhelm the board. Use cantrips to dig for the final piece when resources are low.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when Omniscience is online, you have sufficient mana for a turn, and the board is clear or you can protect your finisher. Commit when you can resolve your threat through the stack or when the opponent has no interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one card, the opponent has a board wipe ready, or you're facing a locked-out control deck with no way to break through. Concede if your enablers are removed and you lack the resources to rebuild your game plan.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use spell-slow, split the stack by casting multiple spells in response to counters, or leverage Omniscience to cast around them. Prioritize cantrips to dig through counters and maintain card advantage.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Disrupt their mana development with discard or early interaction to prevent them from out-accelerating you.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early interaction and efficient removal before going off. Use board wipes or sweepers to clear the board, then transition to your combo game plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply incremental pressure and use spell synergy to out-value their counters. Threaten with multiple angles to force difficult decisions, and use Omniscience to bypass their mana constraints.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value card advantage and protect your enablers. Out-resolve their spells with counterspells or spell-slow, and prioritize graveyard/hand disruption to disrupt their combo.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate while maintaining a balance between interaction and your combo pieces. Against spell-heavy decks, prioritize counterspells and spell-slow. Against landfall, bring in board wipes and artifact/enchantment removal. Maintain a flexible mana base and ensure you have enough early interaction to stabilize.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting early without Omniscience, mismanaging mana for the enabler, and ignoring graveyard hate. Unique interactions: Omniscience combined with spell triggers can create massive value, but be wary of format-specific rules about spell stacking. Practice stack management and learn to read opponent's mana holdings to optimize your plays.

philosophy: Boros Burn is a fast, aggressive deck that wins by combining efficient one- and two-mana creatures with direct damage spells to race opponents to zero life before they can stabilize. It excels against slow, combo-heavy, or control decks that fail to establish immediate board presence, while struggling against decks that can outpace its damage output or lock the board.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and a one-mana play (creature or burn). Keep two lands if paired with a two-mana threat or efficient interaction like Boros Charm. Mulligan hands with no lands, three or more lands, or only expensive spells (>2 mana) with no early pressure. Prioritize hands that can execute a turn 1 creature or burn spell.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a one-mana creature or burn spell. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a two-mana threat or interaction. If opening with two lands, play both, then cast your best two-mana play. Always prioritize establishing board presence over hoarding mana; the deck wins by dealing damage, not surviving.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Control: Bring in board wipes or efficient removal (e.g., Swords to Plowshares, Path to Exile) and life gain if available. vs Combo/Ramp: Bring in additional direct damage, artifact/enchantment hate (Hexing Squelcher is excellent here), and discard. vs Mirror: Bring in additional interaction, counters to key threats, and potentially more burn to out-value the race.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource to convert into damage, not a pool to hoard. Keep one mana open for interaction if facing control, but commit aggressively against non-interactive decks. Manage hand size by prioritizing high-impact spells; discard redundant burn if you have lethal in hand. Graveyard is generally irrelevant for Boros Burn, so don't worry about it unless facing specific graveyard strategies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit all threats when the opponent has no removal or counterspells. Hold back a creature or two if you suspect targeted removal or a sweep, but remember that Boros Burn often wins by out-damaging removal. Use Boros Charm flexibly: 2 life to draw a card, 1 damage to kill a creature, or 2 damage to your opponent.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up mana or cast threats in response to land drops), targeted removal (save creatures for when they're tapped or use charm to kill), and sweepers (have burn ready to close the game before or after the wipe). Pay attention to opponent's life total and card advantage engines.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Direct damage spells and unblocked creatures. The goal is to reach 10-12 damage in a single turn or over two turns. Slickshot Show-Off can provide immediate burst damage or finish the game on entry.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have lethal damage, when the opponent has no interaction, or when you're ahead in the race and they have no way to stabilize. If you have a lethal combination in hand, play it immediately.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to single-digit life with no threats, no burn, and the opponent has a stable board or card advantage engine. Also concede if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., mass counterspells, infinite combo) with no way to break it.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast threats in response to land drops or when you can't be countered. Use Boros Charm to draw cards or deal damage around counters. If facing heavy counters, consider more uncounterable threats or discard to remove the spell in response.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Outpace them. Play as fast as possible. Use direct damage to interrupt their ramp spells or kill their early threats. Hexing Squelcher is excellent here to shut down mana rocks or enchantments before they can snowball.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with efficient removal and life gain if available. Use Boros Charm's life gain to survive the early turns, then pivot to closing the game with your own threats. Don't overcommit to trading; focus on surviving until you can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Force them to use resources on your threats. Use Boros Charm to draw cards and maintain hand size. If they try to stabilize, have burn ready to close the game. Don't let them get comfortable; keep the pressure on.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race. Focus on card advantage and efficient interaction. Use Boros Charm to draw cards and deal damage. Prioritize killing their threats before they can block or trade. If you're ahead, go all-in; if behind, look for ways to disrupt their game plan and stabilize.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. If facing more control, bring in more card draw and interaction. If facing more aggro, bring in removal and life gain. Always keep your core burn and threat package intact. Remember that sideboarding is about adapting to the specific matchups, so track what's working and adjust accordingly.
notes: Common mistake: hoarding mana or holding back threats out of fear of removal. Boros Burn wins by dealing damage, not surviving. Another mistake: misusing Boros Charm. Prioritize the 2 damage to opponent or 1 damage to creature over drawing a card unless you're desperate for cards. Hexing Squelcher is a versatile tool; use it to shut down key permanents or as a 2/2 body. Always calculate damage carefully; one miscounted burn spell can cost you the game.

philosophy: A tempo-midrange shell that leverages cheap, evasive Elementals and instant-speed pumps to out-value opponents in the midgame, transitioning into Ashling, the Extinguisher as a versatile finisher and removal tool. It wins by efficiently trading up into larger threats, out-resourcing control/combo with card draw, and closing games with unblockable damage or targeted removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably including a dual or shockland for fixing), at least one 1-2 mana Elemental (Rekindled Zephyr, Izzet Staticaster), and a pump spell or Ashling. Mulligan to 4-5 lands if you have no early threats, or hands consisting solely of 3+ mana spells. Prioritize hands that can play a land and cast a threat on turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a 1-mana Elemental if open. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-mana Elemental or pump spell. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Ashling (RR) by turn 3-4. Avoid overextending on turn 2; establish board presence while preserving mana for interaction or Ashling's flash.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +1 Ashling, +2 Lightning Strike/Incinerate, -2 Wistfulness, -1 Vibrance. vs Control/Combo: +2 Negate/Spell Pierce, +1 Ashling, -2 early Elementals, -1 pump spell. vs Mirror: +1 Ashling, +2 Negate, -2 early Elementals. vs Ramp: +1 Ashling, +2 Lightning Strike, -2 Wistfulness, -1 Vibrance.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Wistfulness and Vibrance as tempo tools, not just damage spells. Use them to trade your Elementals into larger blockers or clear key removal. Manage Ashling's activation economy carefully; don't over-sacrifice Elementals if you need them for board presence or to trigger death triggers for card draw.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or pump ready to handle blockers. Hold back if facing sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift) or large finishers. Prioritize trading your cheap Elementals into their bigger ones using pumps, saving your best threats for when they're tapped out or out of interaction.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal (Path to Exile, Fatal Push), and combo wincons. Note when opponents tap out for big spells; that's your window to push damage or cast Ashling. Track how many removal spells they've already answered, as Ashling and pumped Elementals become significantly more dangerous once their interaction is depleted.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ashling, the Extinguisher (activated ability or pump-boosted), or a fully pumped Elemental with flying/trample. Win by trading up into their finishers and closing with unblockable damage or direct removal. Use Ashling's flash to respond to their win attempts.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, removal for their threats, and they're tapped out or low on life. Go all-in when Ashling is on board and you can safely activate her or pump a threat past their blockers. Commit when you've forced them to use multiple spells for one threat and you still have resources left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to a single land, have no removal, and they have a board of 4+ creatures. Also fold if they have a combo piece you can't interact with and you're out of card draw, or if you're facing a sweepers-only control shell with no way to rebuild.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Wistfulness/Vibrance on your own Elementals to trade into their blockers, then cast Ashling or threats in response to counterspells. Use cheap threats to force counterspells, then follow up with Ashling or a pump spell. Don't overcommit to the stack; let them counter your first threat before going for the kill.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their land drops. Use Ashling to remove their ramp targets or finishers. Don't overcommit to the board; trade efficiently and use pump spells to clear their early threats. Force them to use removal on your cheap Elementals, then close with Ashling.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Ashling's removal and pump spells to trade up. Mulligan aggressively for early threats and interaction. Use Wistfulness/Vibrance to clear their board or finish them off. Prioritize survival over tempo in the first two turns, then pivot to closing out the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw from pumps and Ashling. Force them to use multiple spells for one threat. Push damage while they're locked out of interaction. Use Ashling's flash to respond to their sweepers or removal, then follow up with a pumped threat.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize keeping Ashling, as her activated ability and flash make her superior. Trade Elementals efficiently and use Negate to protect your finishers. Control the stack by holding priority and responding to their Ashling activations with your own.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards a more control-oriented midrange shell. Increase interaction (Negate, Lightning Strike) and reduce early-game filler. Tailor Ashling counts based on matchup (more vs aggro/control, fewer vs mirror). Always ensure your mana base can support Ashling's RR cost, and consider adding dual lands if you're running into color screw against specific archetypes.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing pump spells on unblockable threats instead of using them to trade into larger blockers or clear removal. Remember that Wistfulness/Vibrance draw a card if the creature dies, so trading is often better than pushing unblocked damage. Ashling's flash allows you to hold priority and respond to their plays. Don't forget to check for hexproof/indestructible threats that Ashling can't target, and be mindful of the sorcery timing of Vibrance in fast matchups.

philosophy: Temur Harmonizer is a tempo-combo hybrid that leverages card advantage and spell duplication to out-value opponents, aiming to dig through its deck to assemble a game-winning threat like Full Bore while disrupting opponent strategies with efficient interaction. It excels against control and combo decks that rely on single-point answers, but struggles against fast aggro and highly resilient landfall strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or shocklands) plus either Consult the Star Charts, Mightform Harmonizer, or a turn-2 play. Mulligan on 1-land hands, hands with only cheap creatures and no answers, or hands with multiple copies of the same low-impact spell. Prioritize hands that can cast Consult the Star Charts on turn 2 or Mightform Harmonizer on turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, potentially cast a cheap cantrip or threat if available. Turn 2: Cast Consult the Star Charts to dig for key pieces or threats, or play a 2-drop creature to apply early pressure. Turn 3: Cast Mightform Harmonizer to copy Consult the Star Charts or a key instant/sorcery, or drop Full Bore if you have the mana and have dug for it. Sequence lands to ensure U/R/G availability by turn 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Control: Bring in additional counterspells, discard, or hate bears; remove some land disruption. Against Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in graveyard hate, artifact destruction, and targeted removal for lands; reduce card draw. Against Aggro/Prowess: Bring in more early interaction, board wipes, or efficient removal; reduce high-cost finishers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction and finishers; don't overextend into sweepers. Use Consult the Star Charts to filter for threats or answers based on board state. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary spell duplication if the opponent has graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or a copy effect ready to protect them. Hold back on casting Mightform Harmonizer if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Prioritize board presence over digging if you're behind.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents save removal for your key creatures. Track their mana development to predict when they can answer your threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Full Bore is the primary win condition, often supported by Mightform Harmonizer copying Consult the Star Charts to dig for it or protect it. Once you have a Full Bore on the board with protection or a board clear, commit to attacking.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a Full Bore, sufficient removal to handle their interaction, and no sweepers in their graveyard or hand. Also commit when you've out-valued them and they have no answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no way to break through, or if you're against aggro with no early interaction and no comeback threats. Also concede if you're down to a dead hand with no lands or answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split casts, cheap cantrips to probe, or cast Mightform Harmonizer to copy Consult the Star Charts after they counter the first. Use mana dorks or ramp to cast threats at instant speed when they're out of mana.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use targeted removal on key ramp creatures. Sideboard in artifact destruction and graveyard hate.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and board wipes. Use Mightform Harmonizer to copy removal or counterspells. Hold back on digging until you stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and threats. Use Consult the Star Charts to find answers to their control spells. Pressure them to force mistakes and avoid letting them stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by playing more efficiently. Prioritize keeping lands and early interaction. Use Mightform Harmonizer to copy their Consult the Star Charts or threats for value. Sideboard in additional hate and removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve by removing high-cost finishers against aggro and adding early interaction. Against control, bring in more card advantage and protection. Against landfall, bring in artifact destruction and graveyard hate. Always keep a balance of interaction and threats based on the metagame.
notes: Common mistake: Over-digging with Consult the Star Charts when you're behind on the board. Always weigh the risk of leaving yourself open to sweepers. Mightform Harmonizer's copy effect is best used on Consult the Star Charts for value or on key instant/sorcery for protection. Watch for opponent's sideboard cards like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void.

philosophy: Simic Landfall accelerates into a dense board of lands and synergistic creatures that trigger on landfall, generating incremental value, card advantage, and combat presence to outlast or overwhelm opponents. It excels against slower, resource-starved decks and control lists that struggle to answer a rapidly growing board of evasive or resilient threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands and at least one landfall creature (Badgermole Cub, Earthbender Ascension, or Mightform Harmonizer). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no creatures, or hands with multiple spells but only 1-2 lands. Prioritize keeping hands that can play a land and a creature on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. Turn 2: Play a second land, then drop a landfall creature if you have the mana (Badgermole Cub can be cast on turn 2 with ramp or a two-land start). Sequence lands first to trigger landfall immediately. Use Mightform Harmonizer's draw engine early to dig for more threats or interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Spells/Control: Bring in counterspells, protection spells, or discard; remove land disruption. vs Aggro (Mono-Green/other aggro): Bring in removal, early threats, or life gain. vs Mirror: Bring in card draw, additional landfall synergies, or targeted removal. vs Ramp: Bring in graveyard hate or artifact/enchantment removal if applicable.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance land drops with creature deployment. Never overextend into a sweep if you lack answers. Use card draw from Mightform Harmonizer to maintain hand size. Prioritize keeping lands in play to fuel future triggers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have landfall triggers to mitigate damage or gain advantage. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use Earthbender Ascension's tap ability to trade or finish low-toughness creatures.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Izzet), sweepers (Wrath of God variants), and fast aggro threats. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up interaction. Track graveyard if they're trying to combo off.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Multiple landfall creatures attacking simultaneously, leveraging Mightform Harmonizer's card draw to find additional threats or combat tricks. Earthbender Ascension can clear blockers or finish the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ lands in play, multiple landfall creatures on board, and no clear interaction in hand. Also commit when the opponent is at low life or has no sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 lands with no creatures, the opponent has a guaranteed win condition (e.g., combo or massive board), or you're facing a hard counter matchup with no sideboard answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost threats to force counter-sacrifices. Play around counters by holding up interaction or using instant-speed land drops if available. Leverage landfall triggers that resolve independently.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on key ramp creatures. Out-value them with card draw and incremental landfall triggers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early landfall creatures to trade efficiently. Use removal on key threats. Stabilize by turning lands into blockers or using Earthbender Ascension to clear the board.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure the board to force interaction. Use card draw to out-resource them. Exploit landfall triggers to generate value faster than they can answer.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw (Mightform Harmonizer) and efficient land drops. Trade creatures favorably using landfall triggers. Control the board with removal if available.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your game plan to the specific matchup. Against control/spells, reduce land count slightly if needed, add interaction, and prioritize keeping hands with early threats. Against aggro, add early removal and life gain. In the mirror, focus on card advantage and favorable trades. Always keep your core landfall engine intact.
notes: Landfall decks thrive on consistency; avoid mulliganing too aggressively for perfect hands. Remember that land drops are your primary resource—never sacrifice lands for tempo unless necessary. Watch for format-specific land hate (like Field of Ruin or Blood Moon) and sideboard around it. Badgermole Cub's early presence is crucial for applying pressure before the opponent can stabilize.

philosophy: Temur Control aims to outlast the fast, spell-heavy meta by leveraging efficient disruption, card advantage, and flashback value to control the board, then close out games with resilient threats or incremental advantage. It excels against combo and aggressive spell decks by trading resources efficiently and out-grinding them in the late game.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fixing/duals), at least one early interaction (counterspell, removal, or discard), and a draw engine (Stock Up or a flashback enabler). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands with only late-game threats and zero disruption.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap cantrip or early interaction if available. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, deploy a counterspell or removal to handle early threats, or cast Stock Up/flashback spells to stabilize and draw into answers. Prioritize fixing over rushing lands to ensure consistency.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Spells: Bring in additional sweepers, cheap counters, and discard. vs Control/Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional draw, and finishers. vs Landfall: Bring in artifact/enchantment removal, board wipes, and hand disruption. vs Mirror: Bring in additional counterspells and card advantage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana for key counterspells and removal until the opponent commits. Use flashback strategically to recycle high-impact spells without clogging your hand. Manage graveyard resources carefully to enable flashback while avoiding hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to resolution or when the board is stabilized. Hold back on overcommitting to one spell; always keep a counter or removal for the opponent's biggest play.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana bases and land drops to predict spell availability. Track graveyard contents to anticipate flashback threats. Note when opponents hold up mana for multiple turns—they’re likely setting up a combo or big spell.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Deploy resilient mid-to-late game threats (e.g., planeswalkers, flying creatures, or value engines) that are hard to interact with. Use flashback to recur removal or counters to protect your finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you’ve established board control, have card advantage, and the opponent has no clear interaction left. Also commit when you can safely cast a finisher through their remaining mana.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to one life, have no interaction or win conditions, and the opponent is on a clear path to victory. Also concede against unfavorable matchups where your deck’s game plan is completely neutralized.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flashback to cast spells from the graveyard, bypassing counterspells. Employ cheap cantrips to force counters and find answers. Use uncounterable threats or split your spells across turns.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to slow their mana development. Use sweepers and artifact/enchantment removal to neutralize their acceleration. Out-value them with card draw and efficient removal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Deploy early counterspells and removal to stabilize. Use flashback to recycle removal for late-game threats. Don’t overcommit to card draw; prioritize survival until you can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply incremental pressure with resilient threats and cantrips. Force them to use resources on your threats while you draw into answers. Use flashback to recur removal or counters when they commit to a big play.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by out-drawing and out-interacting. Prioritize card advantage and efficient counterspells. Use flashback to recycle key spells and maintain pressure. Track their graveyard to anticipate their flashback threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from reactive to proactive when facing slower decks, and double down on disruption against fast decks. Adjust your land base if needed for specific matchups, and ensure your sideboard cards synergize with your flashback strategy.
notes: Flashback is a double-edged sword; don’t over-rely on it early if it leaves your graveyard vulnerable to hate. Consult the Star Charts and Stock Up provide card advantage, but sequence them carefully to avoid flooding. Against the Izzet/landfall meta, prioritize interaction over card draw in the early game.

philosophy: Bant Gearhulk is a resilient midrange-control deck that uses cheap, high-value creatures and discard to dominate the early game, then transitions to a grindy midgame where graveyard recursion and card advantage engines out-value aggressive and combo strategies. It wins by stabilizing against fast decks, out-resourcing them with superior late-game threats, and leveraging efficient interaction to close out games.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches or duals that can find basics/Nature's Rhythm), a discard spell (Thoughtseize/Inquisition), and a turn-2 play (Badgermole Cub, Quantum Riddler, or cheap removal). Keep hands that can play a land, cast a spell, and have a clear plan for turn 2. Mulligan flood, no lands, or all lands with no early interaction or threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Inquisition on a key threat or combo piece. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a cheap creature (Badgermole Cub/Quantum Riddler) or follow up with removal. If opening with Nature's Rhythm, play it on turn 1 or 2 for mana fixing and potential sacrifice for early damage or mana. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for interaction and avoid tapping out unnecessarily.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Prowess/Spellementals: +2 Lightning Strike, +2 Path to Exile, +1 Teferi's Protection, -1 Nature's Rhythm, -1 Quantum Riddler. Against Mono-Green Landfall: +2 Lightning Strike, +2 Path to Exile, +1 Teferi's Protection, -1 Badgermole Cub, -1 Nature's Rhythm. Against Control/Combo: +2 Leyline of the Void, +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Teferi, Hero of Dominaria, -1 Badgermole Cub, -1 Nature's Rhythm. Against Mirror: +2 Thoughtseize, +2 Inquisition, +1 Teferi's Protection, -1 Nature's Rhythm.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve hand for key interaction (discard/removal) until turn 3-4. Use Nature's Rhythm to fix mana or generate extra mana for threats. Don't overcommit creatures; Bant's creatures are value engines, not pure attackers. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary sacrifices unless it fuels Aethergeode Titan/Gearhulk or enables Quantum Riddler.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal for their response or when you can protect them with Teferi's Protection. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Prioritize trading efficiently to maintain card advantage and avoid falling behind on board position.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's counterspells, sweepers (Teferi's Protection, Lightning Strike, Izzet Charm), and Landfall triggers. Track when they run out of removal or discard. Key tell: If they tap out for a big spell or creature without answering your early threats, they're vulnerable to pressure. Note when they commit to a combo or ramp strategy to adjust your interaction accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Aethergeode Titan or Gearhulk (exiling multiple creatures from graveyard for massive value), Teferi, Hero of Dominaria (card advantage and board wipe), or a well-timed Teferi's Protection on a key threat. Win by out-grinding them with card advantage and efficient removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage in hand, removal for their threats, and they have no sweepers. Also commit when you can safely cast a Gearhulk/Aethergeode Titan with multiple exiled creatures. Go all-in when you can protect your threats with Teferi's Protection or when the opponent is out of interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1-2 lands with no interaction, facing a locked board or combo that can't be answered, or if you've mulliganed to a dead hand against a fast deck with no comeback plan. Fold when you're consistently losing the discard war and have no way to stabilize or close out games.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells early. Cast threats in response to their spells or when they're tapped out. Use Teferi's Protection to protect key threats. Don't overcommit to spells they can counter; use creatures that can be cast at instant speed or in response, and prioritize value engines that can't be easily countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use discard to remove key ramp pieces. Stabilize with removal and Teferi's Protection, then out-value them with card advantage. Don't let them get ahead on mana; trade efficiently and punish their overextension.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep interaction for their threats. Use Nature's Rhythm for mana fixing and early damage. Trade efficiently to survive turn 3-4. Bring in more removal and Teferi's Protection post-sideboard. Don't overcommit to board position early; focus on surviving and out-grinding them.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind them with card advantage engines. Use discard to disrupt their game plan. Pressure with threats they can't efficiently answer. Win by out-resourcing and closing out games with Gearhulk/Aethergeode Titan. Don't let them stabilize; keep applying pressure and force them to use resources inefficiently.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping interaction. Use Teferi's Protection to protect key threats. Trade efficiently and win by out-resourcing with better late-game threats (Aethergeode Titan/Gearhulk). Don't overcommit to board position; focus on card advantage and graveyard value.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a midrange control shell to a more reactive or value-focused plan. Against fast decks, prioritize removal and protection. Against control/combo, bring in graveyard hate and card advantage. Adjust land counts if needed, but keep the core mana base intact. Always keep at least one Nature's Rhythm for flexibility unless facing a specific hate deck. Prioritize keeping interaction and card advantage engines over raw threat count.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting creatures without protection or removal. Don't sacrifice Nature's Rhythm unnecessarily unless it's for mana or early damage. Remember that Badgermole Cub and Quantum Riddler are value engines, not just attackers. Use Teferi's Protection sparingly for maximum impact. Watch for Izzet's sweepers and Landfall triggers. The deck's strength is its resilience and card advantage, so play patiently and out-value opponents. Avoid tapping out for non-essential spells; Bant wins by out-resourcing, not by going wide early.

philosophy: A four-color midrange-recursion archetype that leverages landfall synergies and graveyard cycling to out-value opponents. It aims to stabilize the early game with efficient interaction and cheap threats, then overwhelm with recurring value engines, particularly excelling against landfall and spell-slinging strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or fast lands), a turn-1 or turn-2 landfall enabler or cheap threat like Badgermole Cub, and at least one interaction or card draw spell. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only high-cost spells, or no way to interact with the early game. Hands with Quantum Riddler and a land are keepers if you have a way to cast it or protect it.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap enabler or threat if mana allows. Turn 2: Develop board presence with landfall triggers, dig for consistency, or deploy early interaction. Prioritize playing lands that enter untapped to maintain tempo. Use Nature's Rhythm to trigger landfall effects and stabilize or accelerate your board state.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals: Bring in counterspells, artifact/enchantment removal, and graveyard hate. Against Landfall decks: Bring in sweepers, land destruction, and targeted removal for key enablers. Against Control: Bring in more threats, card advantage, and protection spells. Against Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, recursion hate, and card draw to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for interaction versus board development. Use the graveyard strategically; avoid over-committing to recursion if your board is empty. Prioritize keeping key enablers in play over hoarding cards. Manage your four-color mana carefully to avoid flooding or screwing, especially in slower matchups.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or interaction to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy countermagic. Use landfall triggers to stabilize against aggressive strategies or to close out games. Protect your key recursion engines with counters or protection effects.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up mana for protection or cast at end step), sweepers (save removal for key threats), and graveyard recursion (track exile/graveyard size). Key tell: Opponent tapping out on turn 3-4 often signals a combo or big spell. Track when they use their interaction to predict their next move.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Recurring mid-range threats, landfall-enraged creatures, or direct damage from recursion engines. Win by out-resourcing the opponent or closing with a protected big threat. Use Quantum Riddler to manipulate the board state or recur a key finisher from the graveyard.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have board presence, interaction to clear blockers, and enough resources to follow up. Go all-in if the opponent is tapped out or has no sweepers. Commit when you can safely trigger landfall effects to swing the board state in your favor.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you are down to one life, have no interaction, and the opponent has a proven win condition (e.g., infinite combo, uncounterable finisher). Also fold against dead hands with no lands or answers, or when you are consistently out-valued in a control matchup with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split casting, flash, or end-step spells. Cast threats in response to their interaction, or use recursion to bypass counters. Hold up counterspells for their key spells and use protection effects to ensure your finishers resolve.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use artifact/enchantment removal, and disrupt their mana development with targeted hate. Prioritize removing their mana sources and key enablers before they can stabilize. Use sweepers to clear their board if they commit to a big play.
