threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap interaction, board wipes, and efficient removal. Stabilize by turn 3-4, then transition to value. Use Badgermole Cub to trade efficiently and Nature's Rhythm to generate incremental advantage. Don't over-invest in recursion until you have stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure, use card advantage engines, and protect your threats with counters or protection. Out-value them with recursion and landfall triggers. Force them to use their interaction on your threats rather than their win conditions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw, recursion, and targeted removal. Control the graveyard and force them to over-commit. Use Quantum Riddler to disrupt their recursion chains and win the attrition battle.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific matchup. Against spell-heavy decks, increase counterspells and artifact hate. Against landfall, add sweepers and land destruction. Maintain a balance of removal and card advantage. Adjust your land base if needed, but keep duals for consistency. Always track the metagame share and adjust your sideboard based on the most common strategies.
notes: Common mistake: Over-investing in recursion without board presence. Unique interaction: Quantum Riddler can be used to manipulate probability or recursion chains, so always consider its timing. Always track opponent's graveyard and exile zones. The four-color mana base is fragile; play around dual-slow lands in slower matchups. Practice your mana sequencing to avoid flooding or screwing, especially in the early game.

philosophy: Orzhov Midrange aims to survive the early game through efficient removal and life management, then out-value opponents with resilient threats and card advantage. It excels against fast, tempo, and combo decks by stabilizing, answering key pieces, and closing out games with superior late-game resources.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a turn-1 or turn-2 play (1-drop, removal, or early threat), and at least one answer to common metagame threats. Prioritize hands with 2+ lands if they include a turn-2 play. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only 3+ mana spells, or hands lacking early interaction against Izzet/Aggro.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land, optionally cast a 1-drop or hold removal. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a 2-drop threat or removal. Sequence lands to ensure W/B mana availability for removal and midgame spells. If drawing Bloodletter, aim to have 2+ mana by turn 4-5, but can be cast earlier if needed to stabilize.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in direct damage, counterspell hate, and artifact/enchantment removal. Against Mono-Green/Selesnya Landfall: Bring in board wipes, efficient creature removal, and life gain. Against control: Bring in additional threats, card draw, and protection spells. Against mirror: Bring in more removal and card advantage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for removal versus threat development. Avoid overcommitting to the board if the opponent has sweepers or counters. Use the graveyard strategically, particularly for Bloodletter's sacrifice ability to fuel +1/+1 counters or answer threats. Manage hand size to avoid flooding while maintaining pressure.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready or the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if the opponent has potential sweepers, counters, or ramp. Prioritize answering key metagame threats like Landfall triggers or Prowess stacks. Use removal efficiently to protect your board and control the pace.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana base, hand size, and graveyard. Note if they're playing landfall triggers or prowess triggers. Track if they have sweepers or counterspells in hand. Pay attention to when they tap out for big spells or combo pieces.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Bloodletter of Aclazotz with multiple +1/+1 counters, Unstoppable Slasher, or a combination of removal and a resilient threat. Win by out-resourcing and closing with flying or unblockable threats that are difficult to answer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board advantage, the opponent is low on life, or you've removed their key answers. Commit when you can safely attack with Bloodletter or Slasher without risking a sweep.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no draw spells, or if you're down to 1 life against a combo deck with no answers. Also concede if you're mulliganed to 4 and have no lands or answers, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split your spells across turns, or play around counters by holding up removal. Utilize uncounterable effects if available, and pressure with multiple threats to force difficult decisions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Answer ramp sources early with efficient removal. Don't let them reach their big spells. Use life gain and board control to slow their acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and life gain. Don't overextend. Use Bloodletter's sacrifice ability to fuel counters or answer threats. Prioritize survival over aggression.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats. Don't let them stabilize. Use card draw to out-value them. Play around counterspells by holding up removal or using instant-speed threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by better sequencing, holding up removal for key threats, and out-valuing with Bloodletter's synergy. Prioritize board presence and card advantage. Force the opponent to overcommit or make mistakes.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against fast decks, bring in more early interaction and life gain. Against control, bring in more threats and card draw. Always keep main deck removal and a few sweepers. Adjust land count if needed, and consider bringing in hate cards for specific archetypes.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without answers, mismanaging Bloodletter's counters, and ignoring opponent's mana. Unique interactions: Bloodletter can sacrifice your own creatures to grow, but be careful not to give opponent triggers. Requiting Hex likely counters or returns spells, so use it strategically to protect your board or disrupt opponent combos.

philosophy: This deck aims to stabilize through efficient life gain and combat damage triggers, outlasting aggressive and midrange opponents by turning defense into escalating offense. It wins by leveraging life total math and synergistic creatures to out-value faster, more fragile archetypes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early lifegain source or combat damage enabler, and a clear turn-2 play. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early threats, or 0-1 lands with no lifegain/combat synergies. Keep any hand with Sheltered by Ghosts or Leonardo, Cutting Edge if supported by a land and a cheap creature or pump spell.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Plains, then cast a 1-2 mana lifegain spell or early creature. Turn 2: Deploy Sheltered by Ghosts or Leonardo, Cutting Edge, then attack with pump spells or blockers to trigger life gain. Land sequencing: Prioritize Plains for early white mana, then develop into a balanced mana base. If playing fetch/shock-style lands, sequence them to avoid life loss early when you need it.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals: Bring in artifact/enchantment removal, additional lifegain, and cards that pressure the stack or bypass counters. Against Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in targeted removal for key ramp creatures, sweepers for triggered abilities, and life total pressure. Against Izzet Prowess: Bring in early interaction, additional lifegain, and cards that punish tapped-out turns. Against mirror: Bring in extra combat damage synergies, life gain redundancy, and sideboard cards that win value trades.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoover mana for combat damage triggers and pump spells rather than overcommitting to the board. Track life total carefully; use lifegain to offset damage from spells or combat. If your deck interacts with the graveyard, protect key lifegain sources and avoid unnecessary discards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit creatures when you have active lifegain sources to stabilize life total and trade efficiently. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, heavy removal, or can answer your key synergies. Prioritize trading into threats rather than attacking face when life is below 15.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's cheap removal and stack manipulation, Landfall's ramp triggers, and Prowess's burst turns. Key tells: Opponent tapping extra mana or holding up counterspells suggests a stack play. Multiple untapped lands with no immediate play often signals a Landfall or Prowess turn. Track life total swings to anticipate their win condition.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Leonardo, Cutting Edge + Sheltered by Ghosts + combat damage creates a life gain engine that outpaces most formats. Win by trading efficiently, pumping creatures, and leveraging life total math to outlast opponents. Once life is stable (>18), commit to combat damage and pump spells to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when life is stable, you have multiple creatures or combat damage sources, and the opponent has no sweepers or hard removal in hand. Also commit when you can trigger multiple lifegain sources in a single combat to swing life total decisively.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has established an unanswerable board, your life total is critically low (<5), and you have no lifegain or combat triggers in hand. Also concede if you're facing a combo that can win on the spot and you lack interaction or life total buffer.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats to force counter windows, stack triggers to out-value counters, and rely on non-creature lifegain or instant-speed pumps. Attack around the stack by committing to board presence and leveraging life gain to survive removal.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to force life total trades before they stabilize. Use targeted removal on key ramp sources, stabilize with lifegain, and punish overextended boards with combat damage triggers. Don't let them reach their late-game win condition by trading efficiently.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize lifegain and pump spells to stabilize life total. Trade efficiently, avoid overextending, and use blockers to buy time for your synergies. Once stabilized, shift to combat damage and life gain to out-value their aggressive curve.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with early threats and lifegain to force removal. Use life total math to out-value their removal spells, and win through combat damage once they run out of answers. Don't overcommit to the board if they have sweepers; instead, trade and stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win value trades by prioritizing lifegain synergies and combat damage triggers. Use sideboard cards for extra life gain or pump effects, and track life total carefully. Force the opponent to overextend or make bad trades by leveraging your life gain engine.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, increase lifegain density and combat damage synergies against control and mirror matchups. Against aggro and landfall, bring in sweepers, targeted removal, and life total pressure. Generally, adjust based on metagame trends: if Izzet variants dominate, add stack interaction and life gain redundancy; if Landfall is prevalent, add early removal and sweepers. Always prioritize cards that stabilize life total and enable combat damage.
notes: Common mistakes include overextending without lifegain sources, ignoring life total math, and failing to track opponent's removal or counters. Unique interactions: Sheltered by Ghosts + pump spells = massive life gain in a single combat. Leonardo, Cutting Edge synergizes with combat damage and life gain triggers. Watch for format-specific hate cards like artifact/enchantment removal or sweepers. Always calculate life total swings before attacking, and prioritize trading into threats over attacking face when life is below 15.

philosophy: Sultai Reanimator seeks to disrupt the early game with cheap discard and draw spells, then pivot to a graveyard engine to resurrect high-impact threats like Superior Spider-Man and Overlord of the Balemurk. It excels against slow, resource-heavy control and combo decks that overcommit to interaction, while struggling against fast aggro and landfall strategies that can race or establish early board dominance.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land, a turn-1 discard spell (e.g., Deceit), and a graveyard enabler or threat. Mulligan hands with two or more lands, no early disruption, or only reanimation spells without a threat or graveyard setup. Prioritize keeping 3-4 lands if you have a turn-1 discard and a tutor/graveyard enabler, as you need to cast disruption immediately to protect your game plan.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Deceit to disrupt the opponent's hand or set up your graveyard. Turn 2: Play a second land, dig for your reanimation engine or primary threat. If you have a threat in hand and a reanimation spell in the graveyard, sequence mana to cast the reanimation spell on turn 2 or 3. Land sequencing: Start with untapped dual or shock lands when possible to ensure turn-1 disruption, but prioritize basic lands if they guarantee you can cast your early spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Landfall: Bring in graveyard hate, early removal, and counterspells; take out redundant reanimation spells and high-cost finishers. vs Control/Spells: Bring in additional discard, card advantage, and protection for your graveyard; take out aggressive threats. vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate and hand disruption; take out redundant tutors. vs Mirror: Bring in extra graveyard hate and targeted removal; take out generic draw spells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat graveyard resources as a shared pool. Don't overcommit to reanimating low-impact threats if you can save the mana for a game-winning threat. Keep 2-3 cards in hand to answer unexpected threats or protect your graveyard. Mana should be hoarded for reanimation windows, but spent on disruption if the opponent is ahead or developing a threatening board.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have graveyard redundancy or can protect them with counterspells/removal. Hold back if the opponent has board wipes, graveyard hate, or significant removal. Use your discard spells to force the opponent to break their own resources or reveal key combo pieces, then reanimate when they are tapped out or out of answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (exile effects, discard spells targeting your graveyard), board wipes, and counterspells. Note when the opponent starts playing around your reanimation (e.g., holding up removal, playing exile effects). Track their mana development to predict when they can answer your threats, and watch for landfall triggers or elemental synergies that could swing the board quickly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Superior Spider-Man and Overlord of the Balemurk are your primary win conditions. Reanimate them when you have a clear path to damage or when the opponent cannot interact. Use discard spells to clear the way or force the opponent to discard key answers, then close the game with unblockable or resilient threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a reanimated threat on the board, the opponent has no graveyard hate or board wipes in play, and you have enough mana to protect your threat or close the game. Also commit if you can win before the opponent's next turn or if they are tapped out from trying to stabilize against your early pressure.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you have no graveyard resources, no way to dig for them, and the opponent is ahead on board or has established a lock. Concede if you're facing a fast combo or aggro deck that can race you, or if you're out of answers to their graveyard hate/board wipes and have no comeback plan.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard spells to force counterspells out of hand, then cast your reanimation spells when they're empty. Sequence your plays to make the opponent guess, and use cheap threats to pressure them into using counters on non-critical spells. If they counter your reanimation, pivot to digging for backup threats or graveyard hate.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and cheap threats. Don't let them stabilize; use graveyard hate if available, or race them with reanimated threats. Prioritize disrupting their mana development with hand disruption, and be ready to answer their large early threats with removal or counterspells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early discard and removal. Don't overcommit to reanimation until you have a board presence or can answer their threats. Use your graveyard as a resource to dig for answers, and close the game quickly once stabilized. If they go wide, prioritize board wipes or targeted removal to protect your life total.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage and graveyard recursion. Force them to use resources on your threats, then reanimate bigger threats when they're tapped out. Use discard to disrupt their combo or lock pieces, and be patient if they have board wipes; wait for them to use them on your early threats before committing your finishers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the graveyard war. Prioritize graveyard hate and targeted removal. Force the opponent to break their own resources with discard, and protect your graveyard with counterspells or protection spells. Win by out-resourcing their graveyard, answering their reanimation threats, or forcing them to mulligan into a dead hand.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure reanimator shell to a hybrid control/reanimator strategy. Bring in more interaction and graveyard hate against aggressive or combo decks, and keep your core reanimation package against control or mirror matches. Adjust your land base if needed, and ensure you have enough answers to handle the specific threats in the new metagame landscape. Always prioritize keeping your graveyard engine intact while adding matchup-specific disruption.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to reanimating low-impact threats early, leaving you vulnerable to board wipes or graveyard hate. Always keep a backup plan in hand. Unique interaction: Deceit can be used to both disrupt the opponent and set up your own graveyard, so sequence it carefully to maximize value. Superior Spider-Man and Overlord of the Balemurk likely have synergies with your graveyard engine; test their specific interactions to maximize value. In an unknown format, always be prepared to adapt your sideboard based on the actual metagame shifts, and track opponent play patterns to predict when they will interact with your graveyard.

philosophy: Gruul Leyline Aggro aims to deploy a fast, resilient creature swarm powered by Leyline of Resonance, using Slickshot Show-Off and Emberheart Challenger to copy pump spells or combat tricks for explosive, unblockable damage. It wins by overwhelming control and midrange decks before they can stabilize, leveraging speed, mana acceleration, and uncounterable threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one Leyline of Resonance (if opening with three copies), and 2+ cheap creatures or pump spells. Mulligan if you have zero to one lands, only high-cost threats, or no early game interaction/pump spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap creature or mana accelerator. Turn 2: Drop a second land, cast a pump spell or second creature. If Leyline is in your opening hand, play it immediately to establish the board state. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your turn-2/3 threats without flooding. Prioritize playing creatures that can attack or set up Slickshot/Emberheart targets.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Control: Bring in direct damage, additional pump spells, and discard/hate. Against Landfall/Aggro: Bring in spot removal, additional early creatures, and Leyline copies if not open. Against Mirror: Keep Leyline, bring in additional pump spells and removal to win combat math.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for pump spells and protection. Don't overextend into sweepers. Use graveyard strategically if cards interact with it, but prioritize hand resources for combat tricks.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have pump spells or protection ready. Hold back if the opponent has potential sweepers or heavy removal. Use Slickshot Show-Off to copy pump spells on your biggest threats to swing for lethal or stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and targeted removal. Note when opponents tap out for spells vs. creatures. Track graveyard content if they run recursion or combo.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Leyline of Resonance + multiple pumped creatures, often amplified by Slickshot Show-Off or Emberheart Challenger copying a massive pump spell. Direct damage spells to close out.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have Leyline resolved, enough mana for a pump + attack, and the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal in hand. Go all-in if you can swing for lethal or leave them at 0-1 life.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a hard lock, have no removal for their sweepers, Leyline is dead, and you have no way to break through. Also concede if you're down to one card and they have a guaranteed sweeper/removal combo.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats to force counter decisions. Copy pump spells with Emberheart/Slickshot to bypass counters. Use direct damage to pressure through.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum early pressure. Don't let them stabilize. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Leyline makes your threats resilient to their late-game finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use spot removal and pump spells to stabilize. Don't overcommit creatures. Use Leyline to make your blockers unkillable.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Don't give them time to draw into answers. Use Slickshot/Emberheart to copy pump spells on attack to bypass removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win on combat math. Prioritize keeping Leyline. Use additional pump spells and removal to out-value. Track their hand for Leyline copies.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggression to a hybrid control-aggro game plan. Bring in targeted removal and spot protection to handle specific threats. Adjust land counts if the format demands more duals or basics. Prioritize keeping Leyline of Resonance in the main deck if you have 3 copies, and adjust your early game to focus on protecting it and establishing a board.
notes: Leyline of Resonance must be in your opening hand to start in play; otherwise, it's a sideboard card. Slickshot Show-Off and Emberheart Challenger synergize best when you have a specific pump spell or ability to copy—plan your mana accordingly. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection, as control decks can sweep. Watch for format-specific hate cards that target Leyline or creature types.

philosophy: 4c Artifacts leverages mana acceleration, artifact synergies, and high-impact threats to outvalue faster, tempo-oriented decks. It aims to stabilize early, ramp into game-winning artifact enablers, and close out games with resilient board presence or explosive finishers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetches, duals, or artifact-producing lands like Repurposing Bay), plus at least one mana accelerator or artifact tutor. Drop hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, or hands with multiple colors but no ramp/tutors. Prioritize keeping hands that can execute a turn-1 or turn-2 artifact that generates value, mana, or early board presence.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then cast a cheap artifact or mana accelerator. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, cast a second ramp or early threat, and ensure you have the mana to hit key thresholds. Sequence lands to minimize tapping issues; prioritize fetching/dualing into artifact-producing lands early to fuel Repurposing Bay and United Battlefront. Avoid overcommitting to one-mana artifacts if you cannot follow up with your key spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in artifact hate, additional counterspells or countersuit protection, and card draw. Against Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in board wipes, artifact destruction, and early interaction. Against control/mirror: Bring in more card advantage, finishers, and protection spells. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction and key thresholds; avoid overcommitting to low-cost artifacts if you need to cast Simulacrum Synthesizer or United Battlefront. Use artifact synergies to generate card advantage and mana acceleration. Manage your graveyard by protecting key artifacts from discard or sacrifice effects, and recycle artifacts when possible to maintain hand size.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or can follow up with another artifact. Hold back on casting high-value artifacts if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Prioritize establishing a board presence with low-cost artifacts that can be sacrificed or recycled for value, and use your artifacts as blockers to stabilize against aggressive strategies.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, and counterspells. Track opponent's mana development and whether they're going for a combo or midrange plan. Note when they tap out for a key spell, as this is your window to attack or cast a game-winning artifact. Pay attention to graveyard activity, as some meta decks will try to exile or sacrifice your artifacts.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Simulacrum Synthesizer as a primary win condition, supported by United Battlefront for protection or board-wide value. Secondary finishers include high-impact artifact spells or combo pieces that can close the game in one turn. Use your artifact tokens and synergies to overwhelm opponents who lack sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection for your finisher, enough mana to cast it and still have interaction, or when the opponent is tapped out with no sweepers. Also commit when you can follow up with a second threat or card draw to close out the game. Do not commit if the opponent has unanswered removal or a sweeper in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that cannot be interrupted, if you're dead to a board wipe with no recovery, or if you're consistently drawing lands with no artifacts or interaction. Also concede if the opponent has established an unbreakable board and you lack the tools to clear it, or if your sideboard plan was completely outclassed.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use artifact spells that are hard to counter (e.g., mana rocks, lands, or spells with protection), split your casts across multiple turns, and use counterspells or countersuit protection. Prioritize casting threats when the opponent is tapped out or has already used their counters. Force them to choose between countering your ramp or your threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap artifacts to disrupt their mana development. Use artifact destruction and sweepers to reset their board. Out-value them with card draw and resilient threats. Do not let them establish a massive mana advantage; trade efficiently and punish overextended boards.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and artifact-based removal. Stabilize with cheap blockers or protection spells. Transition to a control plan until you can cast your finishers. Use your artifacts as blockers and trade efficiently to survive the early game, then out-value them in the midgame.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with artifact threats and card draw. Force them to use removal on multiple targets. Out-grind them with value engines and late-game finishers. Use your mana base to cast spells at optimal times, and avoid overcommitting to one- mana artifacts that can be easily removed.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by drawing more cards and protecting your key artifacts. Prioritize targeting their finishers and disrupting their mana development. Use sideboard cards that give you an edge in the late game, and be willing to trade artifacts efficiently to maintain board presence. Control the tempo by playing around their counters and sweepers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against faster decks, increase early interaction and artifact hate. Against slower decks, add more finishers and card draw. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards, and consider bringing in versatile answers that work across multiple matchups. Track opponent sideboard cards and be ready to pivot your game plan if they bring in heavy artifact hate or sweepers.
notes: 4c Artifacts relies heavily on consistency and mana base quality. Avoid overcommitting to one-mana artifacts if you cannot cast your key spells. Watch for opponent sideboard cards that target artifacts, and be ready to pivot to non-artifact threats if necessary. Practice sequencing your mana to ensure you can hit thresholds without tapping out prematurely. Remember that artifact synergies often provide incremental value, so prioritize plays that generate multiple benefits per mana spent.

philosophy: Mardu Monument is a midrange-control hybrid that leverages resilient artifact engines to accumulate incremental value while using efficient removal, combat tricks, and evasive threats to outlast fast spell-based and landfall strategies. It aims to stabilize the early game, protect its value engines, and close out games through sustained pressure and superior resource conversion.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including duals or shocklands if applicable), at least one turn-1 or turn-2 play (creature, cantrip, or early interaction), and a clear path to deploying a Monument by turn 3. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, 2+ lands with only late-game threats and no survival tools, or hands with multiple colored mana requirements but no way to cast them before turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land; cast a cheap cantrip or early threat if mana allows. Turn 2: Deploy a Monument if you have the mana, or play a midrange creature/removal. Sequence lands to ensure W/B/R availability by turn 2-3; prioritize playing a land if you hold a turn-2 play that requires it. Use early interaction only to protect your board or answer immediate threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Spells/Spellementals/Prowess: Bring in artifact hate, counterspell disruption, and direct damage. vs Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: Bring in board wipes, targeted removal for key lands/creatures, and enchantment hate. vs Control/Mirror: Bring in additional value engines, card advantage, and protection spells. vs Aggro: Bring in early interaction, combat tricks, and board wipes.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until threats are established or the board is stabilized. Use cantrips to dig for answers or finishers rather than overcommitting to the stack. Manage the graveyard carefully; avoid unnecessary sacrifice outlets unless necessary, as Monuments may interact with it or benefit from controlled recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or combat tricks to protect them. Hold back key finishers if the opponent has unblocked removal or counterspells. Use Monuments as secondary threats or value generators while primary threats trade efficiently. Avoid overextending into Izzet's stack; trade creatures and use artifacts to pressure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's stack interaction (counterspells, instant-speed removal, tempo spells). Track Landfall triggers and key land drops to anticipate their power spikes. Note when opponents save removal for specific threats versus holding for your Monuments. Pay attention to graveyard size, as Moonshadow and Monument synergies can shift rapidly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Moonshadow (evasion and recursion), Cool but Rude (combat advantage and disruption), and fully grown Monuments. Win by trading efficiently, then attacking with unblockable or resilient threats. Use combat tricks to bypass removal and close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no interaction left, or you can swing through with protected threats. Commit when a Monument reaches a critical threshold or when you can close the game before the opponent can stabilize. Push damage when you have unblockable threats or when the opponent's life total is low enough to win outright.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single land, have no answers to a locked board, or are facing a combo that can win unconditionally. Concede if you're consistently drawing dead cards against a control deck with no win condition, or if your Monuments are repeatedly removed without replacement. Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has established a dominant board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split casts, stack tricks, and cheap threats that don't rely on a single spell. Attack around counterspells with multiple small threats or use artifacts that are hard to counter. Save interaction for their counterspells and pressure with unblockable or resilient threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their land drops. Use targeted removal on key ramp creatures/lands. Don't overcommit to the board; trade efficiently and win with value engines. Force them to interact with your threats rather than letting them develop their board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and board wipes. Use combat tricks to survive lethal. Stabilize by turn 3-4, then transition to value. Don't overextend into their combat; trade efficiently and win with Monuments and evasive threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure the board consistently to force interaction. Use card advantage to outlast them. Attack with unblockable threats or sacrifice outlets to bypass removal. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than letting them draw into their win conditions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value is king. Trade efficiently, preserve card advantage, and win by out-resourcing. Use protection on key Monuments and threaten with evasive creatures. Force them to interact with your threats rather than letting them develop their board. Win by out-grinding them in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against spell-heavy decks, prioritize artifact hate and counterspell disruption. Against landfall/ramp, bring in board wipes and targeted removal. In mirrors, sideboard in additional value engines and protection. Always keep at least one versatile interaction in the main deck, and avoid overcommitting to matchup-specific cards that leave you vulnerable to unexpected threats.
notes: Monuments are fragile to board wipes; protect them with counters or sacrifice outlets. Moonshadow's recursion can be a double-edged sword; manage graveyard carefully to avoid feeding opponent strategies. Cool but Rude's downside requires careful timing; use it when you can mitigate the drawback or when the combat advantage is decisive. Avoid overextending into Izzet's stack; use artifacts and creatures to pressure. Practice stack management and timing for combat tricks. Remember that Mardu's three-color identity requires careful mana base construction; prioritize dual lands and fetch/shock mechanics if available.

philosophy: Jeskai Artifacts converts spell density into board presence by triggering artifact engines like Repurposing Bay and United Battlefront with cheap instants and sorceries, then amplifying the resulting Clone tokens with artifact synergies and protection. It aims to out-value and out-scale midrange, combo, and control decks by leveraging Jeskai's removal, card advantage, and recursive artifact value to close out games efficiently.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (duals/talismans preferred), at least one of Simulacrum Synthesizer, Repurposing Bay, or United Battlefront, and 2-3 cheap instants/sorceries (e.g., Lightning Strike, Path to Exile, Consider, Opt). Keep hands with mana rocks if paired with a turn-2 play. Mulligan to pure mana rocks, multiple lands with no artifacts, artifact-heavy hands with zero instants/sorceries, or hands lacking early interaction against aggro.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and a talisman/mana rock, or cast a cheap instant/sorcery if you have free mana. Turn 2: Deploy Simulacrum Synthesizer or an artifact engine, or cast a second cheap spell to trigger Repurposing Bay/United Battlefront. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast a turn-2 spell and a turn-3 artifact, or vice versa. Prioritize playing duals/talismans early to enable Jeskai's two-color requirements for removal and card draw.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs. Aggro (Mono-Green Landfall, Izzet Prowess): Bring in Restoration Angel, Lightning Greaves, Path to Exile, Dovin’s Veto, and artifact protection. Remove Fable of the Mirror-Breaker and excess draw. vs. Control/Spells (Izzet Spells, Izzet Spellementals): Bring in Karn, the Great Creator, Engineered Plated Warbeast, Chalice of the Void (if legal), Thoughtcast, and Urabrask the Hidden. Remove cheap instants that get countered. vs. Selesnya Landfall/Midrange: Bring in Karn Liberated, Urabrask the Hidden, Restoration Angel, and Dovin’s Veto. Remove token generators vulnerable to sweepers. vs. Mirror: Bring in Karn, the Great Creator, Engineered Plated Warbeast, Restoration Angel, and Lightning Greaves. Keep Fable and Karn Liberated for value.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve instants/sorceries for engine triggers and removal; avoid overcommitting to casting spells just to make tokens without follow-up. Use card draw (Consider, Opt, Thoughtcast) to cycle into threats or answers. Manage graveyard carefully if playing Karn, the Great Creator or Engineered Plated Warbeast. Track opponent's mana to time your spells when they're tapped out.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or protection ready to handle interaction. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy countermagic. Use Simulacrum Synthesizer's upkeep ability to copy key threats or stabilize against aggro. Prioritize board presence over hand size when ahead, and use Jeskai's targeted removal to clear blockers before attacking.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Supreme Verdict, Cyclonic Rift), counterspells (Spell Pierce, Flusterstorm, Dovin’s Veto), and hate cards (Chalice of the Void, Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void). Track opponent's mana and card count to predict when they can interact. Note when they tap out for a single spell vs. holding up mana, and adjust your spell timing accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully fueled board of Clone tokens backed by artifact pumps (Engineered Plated Warbeast, Arcbound Ravager, Karn, the Great Creator), or a Karn Liberated / Urabrask the Hidden finish. Simulacrum Synthesizer copying a pumped threat or Restoration Angel blinking a key artifact can close games. Jeskai's removal can clear the path for a decisive attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage secured, the opponent has no sweepers/counters in hand, or you've established a board state that forces them to overextend. Commit when you can protect your threats with Lightning Greaves or Restoration Angel, and when your opponent's graveyard/mana is depleted.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Stasis, Rhystic Study + Counterspell lock), have no removal for their win conditions, or are down to dead hands with no artifacts/instants to trigger engines. Concede if the opponent has multiple sweepers and you lack a way to close before the next upkeep, or if you're consistently losing the resource war against a superior matchup.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Chalice of the Void (if applicable), Thoughtcast to dig, or cast spells at instant speed when they're tapped out. Use Simulacrum Synthesizer ETB effect before they can react, or rely on artifact mana rocks that don't target. Cast instants/sorceries in response to their counterspells to trigger Repurposing Bay/United Battlefront before they resolve, turning their interaction into token generation.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Clone tokens. Use Path to Exile or Dovin’s Veto on key ramp pieces. Don't let them stabilize; force them to interact with your board while you develop. Use Jeskai's card draw to out-resource their acceleration and close before they hit their late-game win conditions.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Restoration Angel, Lightning Greaves, and targeted removal. Use Simulacrum Synthesizer to copy a blocker or pump a token. Prioritize survival over combo; use Jeskai's removal to clear threats and stabilize into a token swarm. Convert early survival into late-game value by leveraging artifact recursion.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and artifact synergies. Use Karn, the Great Creator to recur artifacts and generate value. Apply consistent pressure with Clone tokens to force them to use removal on multiple threats. Bypass counters with artifact mana and non-spell wins, and use Jeskai's protection to keep your key pieces on the board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Karn, the Great Creator and Engineered Plated Warbeast for value. Use Restoration Angel and Lightning Greaves to protect key artifacts. Trade efficiently and out-draw them with Consider/Opt. Control the board with Simulacrum Synthesizer copying their key threats, and use Jeskai's removal to clear blockers before attacking.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw engine density to matchup-specific tools. Against control, prioritize artifact recursion and protection. Against aggro, add early interaction and clock-stoppers. Against midrange, bring in sweepers and hate cards. Always ensure your mana base can support the new colors/tools, and adjust your mulligan strategy to account for the new card distribution. Keep your core Jeskai identity intact while tailoring your interaction and finishers to the specific metagame threats.
notes: Be mindful of the 'Clone' token name synergy; it doesn't interact with other token types or generic artifact synergies. Simulacrum Synthesizer's upkeep ability is a powerful tool but requires careful mana management. Repurposing Bay and United Battlefront trigger on any instant/sorcery, so even cheap removal or cantrips are valuable. Avoid overcommitting to casting spells just to make tokens if you lack board presence or protection. Watch for artifact hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Chalice of the Void) and have a plan to pivot to non-artifact threats or direct damage if needed. Jeskai's removal suite is your best friend in close games; don't hoard it unnecessarily.

philosophy: Boros Leyline Aggro seeks to close games quickly by deploying cheap, resilient threats and leveraging flash + graveyard recursion to outpace slower strategies. It wins by applying relentless early pressure, answering key blockers with instant-speed interaction, and out-valuing opponents through efficient trades and surprise attacks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (duals/shock preferred) plus at least two of: Slickshot Show-Off, Emberheart Challenger, Voice of Victory, or cheap instants (Lightning Helix/Shock). Mulligan on 4+ lands with no threats, or 3+ lands with only one threat. Keep 2 lands if paired with two 1-drops or a 1-drop plus an instant.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Slickshot Show-Off. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Emberheart Challenger or another threat. If the opponent plays a 2-power or less creature, flash in Voice of Victory to remove it or protect your board. Sequence lands to guarantee red mana for Emberheart and instants, and use Slickshot's graveyard trigger to cast removal or pump spells for tempo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Spells/Control: +1 Voice of Victory, +2 Lightning Helix, -2 Slickshot Show-Off. vs Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: +2 Path to Exile, +1 Slickshot Show-Off, -2 Emberheart Challenger. vs Mirror/Aggro: +2 Lightning Helix, +1 Voice of Victory, -1 Emberheart Challenger.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve instant-speed interaction for critical turns rather than burning it on low-impact threats. Use Slickshot Show-Off to cast graveyard instants for tempo or removal, but avoid overcommitting to graveyard recursion if the opponent has graveyard hate. Track hand size to ensure you can answer sweepers or counters when needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal for their answers or when the board is clear of sweepers. Hold back if you suspect a board wipe, heavy counter suite, or hand disruption. Use flash to respond to removal on your key threats, and trade efficiently to maintain board presence without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Izzet), sweepers (Mono-Green/Selesnya), and hand disruption. Note if the opponent saves removal for your finishers or if they are holding up mana for a specific answer. Track graveyard contents for Slickshot triggers and be aware of when they run out of removal, signaling a window to go all-in.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emberheart Challenger (often 4/4+ with flying tokens), Slickshot Show-Off + instant pump/removal, and Voice of Victory as a surprise attacker or blocker. Close with unblockable flyers or pumped ground pounders when the opponent has no answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, sweepers, or counterspells in hand or graveyard. Commit when you can swing for lethal or force a trade that leaves you ahead in board state. Push damage when you know they cannot stabilize or draw into a sweep.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has a guaranteed board wipe or sweep combo in hand and you have no answers. Concede if you are down to 1 life with no threats, opponent has 3+ cards in hand with removal, and you have no comeback potential. Avoid chasing low-percentage draws against established control or ramp.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flash (Voice of Victory) to cast after counterspells resolve. Split threats across turns to force multiple counters. Use Slickshot to cast instants from the graveyard to bypass counters, and avoid overcommitting to a single turn.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum early pressure to prevent them from hitting 4+ lands. Use removal on their mana sources if possible, and don't let them stabilize. Trade efficiently to keep them below lethal while you close the game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Voice of Victory to kill their early threats and hold removal for their finishers. Stabilize with blockers, then close with Emberheart. Avoid overextending into their combat tricks or removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly to prevent them from drawing cards. Use Slickshot to cast instants from the graveyard to break through. Force them to use removal on low-value threats and punish when they run out of answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value Slickshot Show-Off and Voice of Victory for tempo. Prioritize graveyard recursion and efficient trades. Use Emberheart to out-value their board and win combat steps by leveraging flying tokens and +1/+1 counters.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggression to tempo/control if facing heavy disruption. Bring in targeted removal and sweepers against control/ramp, and keep the core threat package intact. Adjust land count if needed (usually 24-25 lands) to ensure consistent mana while maintaining pressure. Prioritize cards that answer their specific win conditions while preserving your own game plan.
notes: Slickshot Show-Off's graveyard trigger is conditional ('this turn'), so plan accordingly and avoid wasting it on low-impact spells. Voice of Victory's 'power 2 or less' restriction means it won't kill most mid-game threats, so use it strategically for flash protection or early removal. Emberheart Challenger scales with dragons, so include a few cheap dragons if possible. Avoid overextending into sweepers and always track opponent hand size to know when to commit or hold back.

philosophy: Golgari Ouroboroid is a recursive land-and-graveyard engine that leverages Nature's Rhythm and Ouroboroid synergy to out-value spell-heavy and landfall strategies through relentless card advantage and board recursion. It wins by stabilizing early, out-grinding opponents in midgame resource wars, and closing games with massive land-driven draws or resilient creature threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with at least two lands (preferably duals or fetches if available), Nature's Rhythm, Badgermole Cub, and at least one early creature or removal. Mulligan hands with three or more lands and no early plays, or hands with only instants/sorceries lacking land support. Drop to five if you have a functional two-land start with a key synergistic piece.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Badgermole Cub if mana allows. Turn 2: Drop a second land, then cast Nature's Rhythm or a 2-drop creature. Prioritize land drops to enable landfall triggers and set up Nature's Rhythm's value. Sequence spells to maximize graveyard fuel for Ouroboroid recursion without overcommitting to shuffling prematurely.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Izzet Prowess): Bring in targeted removal, counters, and graveyard hate. vs Control/Spells (Izzet Spells/Spellementals): Bring in card draw, protection, and additional Nature's Rhythm. vs Landfall (Selesnya/Mono-Green): Bring in artifact/enchantment removal and graveyard hate. vs Mirror: Bring in extra recursion, counters, and discard to win the graveyard war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between land drops, spell casting, and graveyard manipulation. Use Nature's Rhythm on yourself when you have 3+ lands and an empty graveyard to stabilize, or on opponents facing graveyard-dependent strategies. Avoid shuffling your graveyard if you need specific cards for recursion or answers. Track opponent graveyard size to anticipate their Nature's Rhythm or combo potential.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have landfall triggers active or sufficient graveyard recursion to replace them. Hold back against sweepers, heavy removal, or when you lack land synergy to recover. Use Badgermole Cub to dig for lands or answers when the board is stale. Prioritize keeping one creature alive to maintain pressure and recursion fuel.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's counterspells and sweepers; they will try to interrupt your landfall or Nature's Rhythm. Note when opponents shuffle their graveyard—that often signals a big draw or combo setup. Track land counts; opponents with 4+ lands are primed for massive turns. Pay attention to when they hold up mana on their turn end, indicating potential counters or instant-speed interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Nature's Rhythm combined with 4+ lands on the battlefield and a healthy graveyard is your primary win condition, often leading to lethal card advantage or overwhelming board presence. Ouroboroid recursion can close games by repeatedly deploying threats or shuffling key pieces back into play. Badgermole Cub can dig for the final land or answer needed to secure the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ lands, a creature on board, and enough graveyard fuel to sustain recursion for at least two turns. Commit when the opponent has no sweepers or counterspells in hand, or when you can safely cast Nature's Rhythm to draw into game-ending threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to basic lands, the opponent has a cleared board with a massive threat, and your graveyard is empty or stripped. Concede if you have no landfall synergy, no recursion fuel, and the opponent is ahead in both board state and card advantage with no clear comeback path.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Play around counters by establishing land advantage first, then casting Nature's Rhythm or creatures in response to their counterspells. Use cheap threats and stack recursion to force them to spend multiple counters. If they have heavy counter suites, prioritize artifact/enchantment removal and graveyard hate to bypass their interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Badgermole Cub and early creatures to disrupt their mana development. Use targeted removal on key ramp creatures before they can stabilize. Avoid trading into their late-game finishers; instead, win the resource war by out-graving them and using Nature's Rhythm to draw past their threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and counters, then leverage Nature's Rhythm to draw into answers and close the game. Use Badgermole Cub to dig for lands and answers while maintaining board presence. Prioritize keeping one resilient creature alive to pressure the opponent and fuel recursion. Avoid overcommitting to the board until you have enough lands to make Nature's Rhythm impactful.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and use Nature's Rhythm to out-draw them. Exploit their lack of interaction with lands by flooding the board with recursive creatures. Force them to use removal on multiple threats, then win the attrition war with graveyard recursion. If they have heavy sweepers, hold back and wait for an opening to cast Nature's Rhythm safely.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the graveyard war by out-sequenceing your opponent and using counters/removal on key creatures. Prioritize keeping your graveyard stocked for Ouroboroid recursion while stripping theirs. Use Nature's Rhythm to draw into more recursion or answers. Track their land count and graveyard size to anticipate their plays and counter their Nature's Rhythm timing.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift toward matchup-specific hate while maintaining core recursion. Increase removal and counters vs. aggro, add protection and card draw vs. control, and bring in extra graveyard hate vs. landfall. Always keep Nature's Rhythm unless facing extreme graveyard hate, and adjust your land count slightly based on whether you need more mana fixing or early interaction. Practice post-sideboard mulliganing to ensure you keep functional hands with the new card composition.
notes: Common mistake: Over-shuffling your graveyard too early, leaving you without recursion fuel. Remember that Nature's Rhythm targets a player, so use it strategically on yourself for stabilization or on opponents facing graveyard synergy. Ouroboroid's recursion is your win condition; don't sacrifice it lightly. Practice stack interaction to maximize Nature's Rhythm and Badgermole Cub value. In Unknown format, always verify specific card rulings and format restrictions before tournament play.

philosophy: Jeskai Lessons aims to overwhelm opponents by casting a high volume of instant and sorcery spells, leveraging Accumulate Wisdom to recycle them from the graveyard and Gran-Gran to generate a relentless storm of flying Spirit tokens. It wins by out-resourcing control and combo decks with card advantage and board presence, while outmaneuvering aggressive strategies through efficient removal and tempo spells.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land, Accumulate Wisdom, or Gran-Gran plus a land. Prioritize hands with 2-3 lands and at least one Lessons card or early spell. Mulligan pure curve-outs, hands with only non-basic lands that enter tapped without fetches, or hands lacking a way to cast Accumulate Wisdom (needs 2U) if you have no cantrips.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap instant to set up the graveyard. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Accumulate Wisdom if possible, or cast Gran-Gran and follow up with instants. If opening with Lessons, tutor for Accumulate Wisdom or a key spell like Stormchaser's Talent and cast it. Mana base should prioritize fetching basics to ensure consistent WUU drops.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in direct damage, spot removal, and artifact/enchantment hate. vs Control/Combo: Bring in counterspell protection, additional recursion, and finishers. vs Ramp: Bring in artifact destruction, graveyard hate, and early interaction. vs Mirror: Bring in additional Lessons, card draw, and targeted removal to disrupt their engine.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a secondary hand; never cast a spell you can't afford to lose unless it's a win-con or essential interaction. Use Lessons to find specific answers or Accumulate Wisdom if it's in the graveyard. Manage mana to keep open for counterspells or removal when needed, but commit to casting spells to fuel Gran-Gran and card draw.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats early if the board is empty, as Gran-Gran scales with spell volume. Hold back if facing sweepers or heavy removal; instead, use instants to disrupt their plays and preserve your engine. Prioritize establishing Accumulate Wisdom before overcommitting to a single board state.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), graveyard hate (e.g., Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), and counterspells. Note when opponents save removal for Accumulate Wisdom or Gran-Gran. Track their mana development to predict when they can cast their win conditions.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully fueled Accumulate Wisdom paired with Gran-Gran and a graveyard full of instants/sorceries creates an unstoppable token storm. Direct damage spells or combo finishers can close out games against control.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Accumulate Wisdom on the battlefield, a full graveyard, and enough mana to cast 3+ spells in a turn. Also commit when your opponent has no sweepers or graveyard hate in play.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard-lock combo with no interaction, if your graveyard is permanently exiled/hated and you have no alternative win conditions, or if you're down to a dead hand with no lands or answers against a stabilized board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split-second spells, cheap cantrips to out-value counters, or cast Accumulate Wisdom in response to their counterspells to protect it. Utilize Lessons to find protection or alternative win conditions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap instants and Gran-Gran. Use artifact destruction and graveyard hate to disrupt their mana rocks and recursion. Don't let them stabilize; trade efficiently and punish their overextended mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize spot removal and board wipes. Use Lessons to find efficient answers to key threats. Gran-Gran can block or trade into early attackers, but focus on stabilizing before going off.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and spell volume. Use Accumulate Wisdom to recycle interaction and threats. Pressure their life total while they're stuck in the late game, and be ready to answer their sweepers with cheap instants.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by casting more spells to trigger Gran-Gran and draw cards. Prioritize Lessons to find Accumulate Wisdom or key interaction. Use targeted removal to eliminate their Gran-Gran or Accumulate Wisdom before they can stabilize.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure combo engine to a more interactive control/storm hybrid. Add more hate cards against the dominant metagame threats (e.g., artifact/enchantment removal, graveyard hate, or additional counters). Adjust your land base if needed, and consider bringing in alternative win conditions like direct damage or combo pieces if your primary engine is targeted.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to casting spells without considering graveyard hate or sweepers. Always keep a land in hand to ensure you can cast Accumulate Wisdom if it's in the graveyard. Stormchaser's Talent synergizes heavily with high spell volume; use it to dig for key pieces or fuel your engine. Remember that Accumulate Wisdom exiles spells cast from the graveyard, so you can only cast each spell once per game unless you have ways to return it to the graveyard.

philosophy: Sultai Excruciator is a midrange-control hybrid that disrupts opponent spellcasting and hand resources while leveraging graveyard recursion to generate tempo and board presence. It aims to out-resource and outmaneuver spell-heavy and ramp strategies by controlling the early game and closing out with resilient threats or recursive spell bursts.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or fetches if available, otherwise basic Swamps, Islands, or Forests), Rakshasa's Bargain, and a turn-2 play (Emeritus of Ideation or Deceit). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early interaction, 0 lands, or hands lacking a way to disrupt the opponent's early game.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and hold up mana for Rakshasa's Bargain or Deceit. Turn 2: Cast Rakshasa's Bargain to disrupt Izzet spell chains or Landfall mana acceleration, or play a cheap threat/land. If drawing Emeritus, sequence with a land drop and hold for graveyard recursion. Avoid overcommitting to the board without answering potential removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Izzet Spells/Prowess: +2 targeted counterspells, +1 hand disruption. vs Selesnya/Mono-Green Landfall: +2 artifact destruction, +1 enchantment hate. vs Control: +1 card draw, +1 direct damage. vs Mirror: +1 targeted removal, +1 graveyard hate. Keep Rakshasa's Bargain and Emeritus as staples unless facing extreme graveyard hate.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction on the opponent's turn. Use Rakshasa's Bargain on high-impact spells (counters, big threats, win conditions). Manage your graveyard by feeding Emeritus when you have a high-value instant or sorcery to cast, but avoid overcommitting if the opponent has graveyard hate or board wipes.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have answered their removal or can stabilize. Hold back if the board is empty and you have disruption; trade up with removal before playing expensive creatures. Use flying tokens from Emeritus to bypass ground blockers and apply pressure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for Izzet's spell chains and prowess triggers. Note Landfall's mana acceleration and token generation. Track graveyard contents for Emeritus targets and potential graveyard hate. Monitor opponent's hand for Rakshasa's Bargain or Deceit to avoid overcommitting to high-value spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emeritus of Ideation casting a high-impact spell (like a board wipe or targeted removal) plus a flying Spirit token, or Rakshasa's Bargain exiling a win condition and replaying it. Direct damage spells and resilient creatures with flying or evasion.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, have answered their removal, and can follow up with a threat or recursive spell. Go all-in when you can close the game in 1-2 turns without leaving yourself vulnerable to a counter-swing or graveyard hate.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you are down to 1-2 cards, facing a combo that cannot be disrupted, or in a dead matchup with no interaction or win condition. Also concede if you are locked out of your mana base or key cards, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Rakshasa's Bargain to exile their counterspells or key spells. Cast Emeritus or threats in response to their counters, or sequence spells to force counters on low-value targets. Utilize uncounterable threats or instant-speed recursion to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early disruption is key. Rakshasa's Bargain on mana rocks or big threats. Board wipes or targeted removal. Do not let them stabilize; pressure early and disrupt their mana development. Use flying tokens to bypass early ground blockers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early interaction (Deceit/Rakshasa's Bargain) to stall. Play efficient removal. Use Emeritus to generate flying tokens for block. Stabilize by turn 4-5, then transition to control. Avoid overcommitting to the board before you have answers.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with card draw and recursion. Use Rakshasa's Bargain to disrupt their hand or graveyard. Apply pressure with threats they cannot answer efficiently. Win through attrition or combo finishers by forcing them to spend resources on removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Prioritize hand disruption and targeted removal. Rakshasa's Bargain is key to exiling their Emeritus or key spells. Win the resource war by managing your graveyard, forcing unfavorable trades, and out-recursing their high-value spells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against spell-heavy decks, increase counterspells and hand disruption. Against ramp/aggro, add early removal and board wipes. Against control, add card advantage and direct damage. Always keep Rakshasa's Bargain and Emeritus as staples unless facing extreme graveyard hate. Adjust your land base if needed to ensure consistent mana for three-color spells.
notes: Avoid overcommitting to Emeritus without a clear graveyard target or follow-up. Rakshasa's Bargain is best used on high-impact spells, not filler. Watch for graveyard hate in control mirrors. Deceit should be saved for critical moments or to disrupt spell chains. Practice sequencing to avoid mana screw/flood, and always prioritize answering win conditions over developing your own board.

philosophy: Temur Leyline Aggro is a fast, pressure-based strategy that overwhelms opponents with cheap, evasive threats and token generation while leveraging Leyline cards as either early board presence or a secondary win condition. It excels against slow, combo-dependent, or control-heavy decks that lack early interaction, but struggles against fast aggro or heavy hate-bear strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing untapped entry or fetchable duals), at least two 1-drops (Slickshot Show-Off, Hired Claw, Elusive Otter), and a Leyline card if it can be cast or protected by turn 2. Mulligan on triple lands, single-card hands, or hands with only 2-drops and no early threats. Prioritize hands that can establish board presence or Leyline advantage by turn 1-2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land, cast Slickshot Show-Off or Elusive Otter if mana allows. If you have a Leyline in opening hand, ensure you can cast it or have it in play by turn 2. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Hired Claw or another 1-drop, attack with evasive threats. Sequence lands to ensure U/R/G availability by turn 2. If you have fetches, chain them to find duals for untapped entry.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Control: Bring in artifact/enchantment hate, additional removal, and more aggressive threats. Against Landfall/Aggro: Bring in board wipes, additional early interaction, and life gain. Against Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional counterspells or discard, and Leyline of Punishment if applicable. Against Mirror: Bring in more card draw, additional flash threats, and targeted removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for flash threats and interaction. Don't overextend into known sweepers. Use Leyline cards strategically—don't rely on them as your only win condition if the board is stale. Manage hand size by trading creatures for removal or using evasion to pressure.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have evasion or when the opponent lacks removal. Hold back if they have sweepers or hard counters. Use Slickshot Show-Off's -1/-1 effect to neutralize key blockers. Use Elusive Otter to bypass large creatures.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Izzet), landfall triggers (Selesnya/Mono-Green), and combo pieces. Key tells: opponent tapping for multiple colors, holding up mana on turn 2-3, or playing non-basic lands that suggest ramp. Track graveyard for recursion or combo pieces.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Overwhelming board presence with evasive 1/1s, Leyline of the Meek tokens, and Slickshot Show-Off's -1/-1 effect clearing blockers. If Leyline is the win con, ensure it stays in play and pressure the opponent to break it or lose to combat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have evasion, the opponent has no sweepers, or you're at lethal in 2-3 turns. Commit when you can trade favorably or when the opponent is low on life.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no interaction, if your hand is dead against the board state, or if you're down to one life and the opponent has a guaranteed win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flash threats, split your casts, or rely on uncounterable effects if available. Use Leyline of Abundance if it helps with mana/landfall to bypass counters. Attack around counterspells with evasive threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to finish them before they stabilize. Use removal for key ramp creatures. Don't let them get to 4+ mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early interaction, hold up removal for their key threats, and stabilize with board wipes if available. Don't overcommit to trading.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure consistently, use evasion to bypass removal, and leverage Leyline as a secondary win condition. Force them to use resources on multiple threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, use flash to surprise, and target their Leylines or key threats. Trade favorably and pressure the life total.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards a more interactive or resilient aggro plan. Against control/spells, bring in more direct damage, counterspells, or artifact/enchantment hate. Against aggro/landfall, bring in sweepers and early interaction. Always keep your core threat package but adjust the supporting cast to handle the metagame's top decks.
notes: Common mistakes include overextending into sweepers, neglecting land sequencing, and relying too heavily on Leyline as a win condition. Remember that Elusive Otter can't be blocked by creatures with power 2+, so use it to trade into large blockers. Slickshot Show-Off's -1/-1 effect is best used on key blockers or combo pieces. Leyline cards are powerful but vulnerable to hate; protect them or have a backup plan.

philosophy: This deck leverages recursive self-bounce to repeatedly trigger enter-the-battlefield effects, generate mana, and draw cards while using Esper's interaction suite to control the board. It aims to out-grind value and tempo matchups by turning disruption into advantage and overwhelming opponents with sustained engine pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for Boomerang Basics and WUB mana), at least one bounce enabler (Stormchaser's Talent or Kaito), and a turn-1 or turn-2 play. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no bounce spells, 1-2 lands with only bounce spells and no early threats, or hands lacking a way to protect your engine.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land; if you have a cheap bounce spell and a Boomerang Basic, consider bouncing it for early mana or card advantage. Turn 2: Deploy Kaito or a bounce enabler, bounce a Boomerang Basic or itself to trigger ETB effects, and establish a resilient board. Sequence lands to ensure WUB availability for interaction on opponent's turn.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono-Green Landfall, Izzet Prowess): Bring in spot removal, counterspells, and direct damage. vs Control/Spells (Izzet Spells): Bring in card advantage, protection spells, and late-game finishers. vs Landfall (Selesnya): Bring in board wipes, artifact/enchantment hate, and bounce lock pieces. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and counters to disrupt recursion loops.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoarded bounce spells for key ETB triggers or to protect Kaito. Use Boomerang Basics for flexible mana acceleration and card advantage. Avoid over-bouncing; prioritize triggers that generate tempo or card advantage. Monitor your graveyard to ensure bounce doesn't inadvertently feed opponent recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only after securing bounce protection or having interaction ready. Use bounce to temporarily remove opponent's key threats while setting up your own triggers. Hold back on committing your entire board if you lack the interaction to handle their response or sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent mana development (ramp vs control), graveyard activity (recursion threats), and board state (when they assemble a win condition). Key tells: Holding up mana on opponent's turn suggests counterspells or instant-speed hate; playing multiple permanents suggests they're going wide or setting up a combo.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kaito, Bane of Nightmares as a protected value engine or combat finisher, repeated Boomerang Basic bounces for lethal mana/card advantage, and Stormchaser's Talent for late-game recursion. Win by overwhelming with triggered value or closing out with a protected threat that cannot be easily removed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have bounce protection, enough mana to survive a turn, and the opponent has no clear answer. Commit when you can trigger multiple ETB effects in response to their removal or when you can close the game before they can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single land with no bounce spells, facing a locked board with no interaction, or against a combo deck that has already assembled its win condition. Also concede if your bounce spells are consistently countered and you have no way to protect your engine or transition to an alternative win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use bounce spells as free spells since they resolve or bounce on resolution. Cast threats at end step, use Kaito's abilities to protect your spells, or bounce their counterspell if possible. Prioritize spells with flash or instant-speed triggers to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction and spot removal. Use bounce to delay their mana rocks and disrupt their acceleration. Bring in board wipes and artifact hate to slow their game plan while you stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and counterspells. Use bounce to temporarily remove aggressive threats while setting up your engine. Don't overcommit to value if you'll die to combat; prioritize survival and early board control.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats that trigger on bounce. Out-value them by generating more cards than they can answer. Use bounce to protect your threats from sweepers and force them to spend resources on multiple triggers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better card advantage and more efficient bounce spells. Prioritize targeting their Kaito or key bounce enablers. Use counterspells to disrupt their recursion loops and force them to replay their engine at a disadvantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure value engine to a more interactive or aggressive stance depending on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, prioritize card draw and protection. Against aggro/ramp, bring in early interaction and hate pieces. Always keep at least two bounce spells and a Kaito copy if possible, as they are core to the game plan, and adjust your land base to ensure consistent WUB mana for sideboarded interaction.
notes: Common mistake: Bouncing permanents without calculating ETB triggers or mana needs. Always ask what value you gain from each bounce. Unique interaction: Boomerang Basics can be bounced for mana/card advantage even when tapped, enabling surprise turns. Kaito's abilities likely synergize with exile/bounce; check timing to maximize value. Avoid over-relying on bounce; maintain a backup win condition and be prepared to pivot to combat if your engine is disrupted.

philosophy: Out-value and out-last opponents by controlling the board, disrupting their game plans, and leveraging superior card advantage to close out games with late-game threats. Excels against spell-heavy and aggressive decks by stabilizing early with Deceit and out-resourcing them in the mid-to-late game with Superior Spider-Man and Perigee Beckoner.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one turn-1 land drop, and early interaction (counters, spot removal, or cantrips). Keep hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1, or two lands and a cantrip. Mulligan to hands that can stabilize against Izzet spells or Landfall ramp, typically requiring at least two lands and a way to interact or draw cards.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction if available. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a mid-cost threat or hold interaction for key Izzet spells/Landfall triggers. Prioritize playing lands to ensure consistent mana for both colors. If opening with a hand that has multiple lands and no early spells, consider a mulligan unless you have a strong plan to stabilize.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Izzet Spells/Spellementals: Bring in additional counterspells, bounce effects, and discard.
